title: Original Legacy Bar Multiple-Choice Drafts - Batch 002
description: Seventy original, AI-assisted draft questions: ten in each of the seven legacy MBE subjects. These are not NCBE questions and are not endorsed by NCBE.

rights.marketingRestriction: Do not call these official, released, licensed, actual, or NCBE questions. Do not imply NCBE affiliation or endorsement.

styleNotes: Use a realistic factual scenario, a distinct call of the question, and four parallel options.
Test application of one principal rule, occasionally with one closely related rule.
Avoid trivia, local law, obscure exceptions, trick wording, and irrelevant cultural knowledge.
Each distractor must reflect a plausible legal error and must be independently explained.
Keep party labels neutral and facts no more detailed than needed to resolve the issue.

questions.subject: Civil Procedure
questions.subtopic: Federal subject-matter jurisdiction - aggregation of claims
questions.stem: A citizen of State A sued a citizen of State B in federal district court. The complaint asserted one state-law claim for $50,000 arising from damage to the plaintiff's car and a second, unrelated state-law claim for $35,000 arising from the defendant's breach of a consulting agreement. The plaintiff alleged both claims in good faith. The defendant moved to dismiss for lack of subject-matter jurisdiction because neither claim, considered separately, exceeds $75,000.
questions.call: Should the court grant the motion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because unrelated claims may never be combined to satisfy the amount-in-controversy requirement.
choices.rationale: A single plaintiff may aggregate multiple claims against a single defendant even when the claims are unrelated.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, because a single plaintiff may aggregate all claims against a single defendant.
choices.rationale: The two good-faith claims total $85,000, so the amount in controversy is satisfied and complete diversity exists.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, because each claim must independently exceed $75,000 when federal jurisdiction is based solely on diversity.
choices.rationale: The jurisdictional amount applies to the aggregate amount properly placed in controversy, not separately to each claim by one plaintiff against one defendant.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, but only because the two claims arise under the supplemental-jurisdiction statute.
choices.rationale: Supplemental jurisdiction is unnecessary because aggregation itself permits the plaintiff to satisfy the diversity amount.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether one plaintiff may aggregate unrelated state-law claims against one diverse defendant to meet the amount in controversy.
explanation.governingRule: A single plaintiff may aggregate all claims against a single defendant, whether related or unrelated, when determining whether the amount in controversy exceeds $75,000.
explanation.application: The plaintiff has two claims against the same defendant totaling $85,000, and the parties are completely diverse. The court therefore has diversity jurisdiction.
explanation.commonTrap: Assuming that every individual claim must exceed $75,000. Aggregation rules depend on the alignment of parties, not on whether the claims share facts.
explanation.memoryAid: One plaintiff plus one defendant: add the claims.

authoring.contentWarning: Verify current law, wording, taxonomy, one-best-answer validity, and distractor quality before publication.

questions.subject: Civil Procedure
questions.subtopic: Venue - substantial part of events or omissions
questions.stem: A driver who resides in the Northern District of State A collided with a pedestrian in the Southern District of State B. The pedestrian, a citizen of State C, suffered all of her injuries in the Southern District of State B. The driver has sufficient contacts with State B for personal jurisdiction arising from the collision. The pedestrian filed a diversity action against the driver in the federal district court for the Southern District of State B. The driver moved to dismiss for improper venue.
questions.call: Should the court grant the motion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because a diversity action may be filed only where the defendant resides.
choices.rationale: The general venue statute also permits venue where a substantial part of the events giving rise to the claim occurred.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because the pedestrian does not reside in the district where she filed the action.
choices.rationale: The plaintiff’s residence is generally not a basis for venue under the facts presented.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because every federal district court in State B is a proper venue whenever the defendant is subject to personal jurisdiction in that state.
choices.rationale: Personal jurisdiction in the state does not automatically make every district in the state a proper venue.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, because a substantial part of the events giving rise to the claim occurred in that district.
choices.rationale: The collision and injury occurred in the Southern District of State B, making venue proper there.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether venue is proper in the federal district where the accident and injury occurred.
explanation.governingRule: Venue is proper in a judicial district in which a substantial part of the events or omissions giving rise to the claim occurred.
explanation.application: The collision and the resulting injury occurred in the Southern District of State B, so that district has a direct and substantial connection to the claim.
explanation.commonTrap: Treating the defendant’s residence as the exclusive venue option.
explanation.memoryAid: Venue follows either the defendants or the events.

authoring.contentWarning: Verify current law, wording, taxonomy, one-best-answer validity, and distractor quality before publication.

questions.subject: Civil Procedure
questions.subtopic: Service of process - service at an individual’s dwelling
questions.stem: A plaintiff filed a federal action against an individual defendant. A process server went to the defendant's usual home and found the defendant's 17-year-old daughter, who lived there permanently and appeared mature and responsible. The process server left a copy of the summons and complaint with the daughter and explained that the papers were for the defendant. The defendant later moved to dismiss for insufficient service of process.
questions.call: Should the court grant the motion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because the papers were left at the defendant’s dwelling with a resident of suitable age and discretion.
choices.rationale: Federal Rule 4 permits substituted service at an individual’s dwelling by leaving the papers with someone of suitable age and discretion who resides there.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, because any person found inside the defendant’s home may accept service for the defendant.
choices.rationale: The recipient must reside at the dwelling and be of suitable age and discretion; mere presence is not enough.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, because substituted service is valid only if the recipient is at least 18 years old.
choices.rationale: The federal rule does not establish a fixed age of 18; it asks whether the resident is of suitable age and discretion.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, because service on an individual is effective only through personal delivery to that individual.
choices.rationale: Personal delivery is one method, but service at the dwelling on a suitable resident is another expressly authorized method.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether leaving process with a mature 17-year-old resident at the defendant’s usual home is valid service.
explanation.governingRule: An individual may be served by leaving the summons and complaint at the individual’s dwelling or usual place of abode with someone of suitable age and discretion who resides there.
explanation.application: The daughter lived at the defendant’s usual home and was sufficiently mature and responsible, so substituted service was effective.
explanation.commonTrap: Assuming that “suitable age” automatically means the age of majority.
explanation.memoryAid: Dwelling plus resident plus suitable age and discretion.

questions.subject: Civil Procedure
questions.subtopic: Amended pleadings - relation back when the wrong defendant was named
questions.stem: A patient timely filed a federal negligence action against “Central Medical Center, Inc.” after surgery at a hospital. The hospital was actually owned and operated by “Central Health System, Inc.,” a related corporation with the same registered agent and legal department. The limitations period expired two weeks after the complaint was filed. Within 60 days after filing, Central Health System received the complaint, knew that it was the intended defendant, and knew that the patient had used the wrong corporate name. The patient then sought leave to amend the complaint to substitute Central Health System.
questions.call: Will the amendment relate back to the date of the original complaint?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because adding a new defendant after the limitations period has expired can never relate back.
choices.rationale: Rule 15 expressly permits relation back for certain amendments changing a party or party name.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, because the patient did not serve Central Health System before the limitations period expired.
choices.rationale: The relevant notice period is generally the Rule 4(m) service period, not necessarily the limitations period itself.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, because Central Health System received timely notice and knew that it was omitted because of a mistake concerning identity.
choices.rationale: The claim is unchanged, notice arrived within the service period, and the intended defendant knew that a naming mistake caused the omission.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, because any amendment arising from the same medical treatment automatically relates back.
choices.rationale: The same transaction is necessary, but changing a defendant also requires timely notice and knowledge of the mistake.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether an amendment substituting the intended corporate defendant relates back after the limitations period.
explanation.governingRule: An amendment changing a defendant relates back when the claim arises from the same conduct, the new defendant received notice within the Rule 4(m) period without prejudice, and the new defendant knew or should have known that it would have been sued but for a mistake concerning identity.
explanation.application: Central Health System had notice within 60 days, shared infrastructure with the named entity, and knew it was the intended defendant. The requirements for relation back are satisfied.
explanation.commonTrap: Focusing only on the expiration of limitations instead of the new defendant’s timely notice and knowledge.
explanation.memoryAid: Same occurrence, timely notice, and known identity mistake.

authoring.contentWarning: Verify current law, wording, taxonomy, one-best-answer validity, and distractor quality before publication.

questions.subject: Civil Procedure
questions.subtopic: Compulsory counterclaims - claims arising from the same transaction
questions.stem: After a two-car collision, one driver sued the other in federal court for property damage. The court had diversity jurisdiction. The defendant knew that she had suffered personal injuries in the same collision but did not assert any counterclaim. The original plaintiff sought $100,000 in property damages, and no jurisdictional defect affected the first action. The property-damage action proceeded to a final judgment after trial. The former defendant then filed a separate federal action against the former plaintiff seeking damages for her personal injuries from the collision.
questions.call: Is the second action barred?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because a personal-injury claim is legally different from a property-damage claim.
choices.rationale: Compulsory-counterclaim analysis turns on whether the claims arise from the same transaction or occurrence, not whether they seek the same type of damages.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because the personal-injury claim was a compulsory counterclaim in the first action.
choices.rationale: The claim existed during the first case and arose from the same collision, so it had to be asserted then.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because a counterclaim requires an independent basis of federal subject-matter jurisdiction.
choices.rationale: A compulsory counterclaim ordinarily falls within supplemental jurisdiction and does not need an independent jurisdictional basis.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, but only if the first jury expressly decided the amount of the former defendant’s personal injuries.
choices.rationale: The bar results from failure to plead a compulsory counterclaim; an express finding on the unasserted damages is unnecessary.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a mature personal-injury claim from the same accident had to be asserted as a compulsory counterclaim.
explanation.governingRule: A pleading must state a counterclaim that the pleader has against an opposing party if the claim arises out of the same transaction or occurrence and does not require an unavailable third party.
explanation.application: Both claims arose from the same collision, and the personal-injury claim was known and mature during the first action. Failure to assert it bars the later action.
explanation.commonTrap: Confusing different categories of damages with different transactions.
explanation.memoryAid: Same accident usually means compulsory counterclaim.

authoring.contentWarning: Verify current law, wording, taxonomy, one-best-answer validity, and distractor quality before publication.

questions.subject: Civil Procedure
questions.subtopic: Summary judgment - nonmovant may not rely on pleadings alone
questions.stem: A customer sued a manufacturer in federal court, alleging that a defect in a kitchen appliance caused a fire. After discovery closed, the manufacturer moved for summary judgment and cited deposition testimony and expert reports showing no evidence that the appliance caused the fire. The customer submitted no affidavits, deposition testimony, expert material, or other evidence. Instead, the customer relied solely on the complaint's allegation that the appliance caused the fire.
questions.call: How should the court rule?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Grant the motion, because the customer has not identified evidence creating a genuine dispute on causation.
choices.rationale: Once the manufacturer showed an absence of evidence on an essential element, the customer had to identify specific record evidence rather than rely on allegations.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Deny the motion, because the complaint itself creates a factual dispute that must be tried.
choices.rationale: At summary judgment, unsupported allegations in a pleading do not create a genuine dispute of material fact.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Deny the motion, because the manufacturer bears the ultimate burden of disproving causation at trial.
choices.rationale: The customer bears the burden of proving causation; the movant may point to the absence of supporting evidence.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Grant the motion only if the manufacturer proves that another identified source caused the fire.
choices.rationale: The movant need not prove an alternative cause when the nonmovant lacks evidence on an essential element.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a plaintiff opposing summary judgment may rely only on allegations after the movant identifies an absence of evidence.
explanation.governingRule: A party opposing summary judgment must cite particular materials showing a genuine dispute of material fact and may not rest solely on allegations or denials in the pleadings.
explanation.application: The customer produced no admissible evidence of causation after discovery. Because causation is essential to the claim, summary judgment is appropriate.
explanation.commonTrap: Assuming that a well-pleaded complaint remains enough after discovery closes.
explanation.memoryAid: Pleadings start the case; evidence survives summary judgment.

questions.subject: Civil Procedure
questions.subtopic: Right to jury trial - mixed legal and equitable claims
questions.stem: A homeowner sued a contractor in federal court. The homeowner sought damages for breach of contract and an injunction requiring the contractor to remove an encroaching structure. Both requests depended on whether the contractor had permission to build on a disputed strip of land. The homeowner timely demanded a jury trial on all issues. The contractor asked the judge to decide the factual issue first as part of the request for an injunction.
questions.call: How should the court proceed?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The judge should decide the common factual issue first because an injunction is an equitable remedy.
choices.rationale: A court may not decide common factual issues in the equitable claim first in a way that defeats the jury right on the legal damages claim.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The jury should decide the common factual issue in connection with the damages claim before the judge resolves the injunction request.
choices.rationale: The Seventh Amendment preserves a jury trial on the legal claim, and common factual issues ordinarily must be tried to the jury first.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The judge should decide every issue because the complaint requests at least one equitable remedy.
choices.rationale: Joining an equitable claim does not eliminate the jury right on a legal claim for damages.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The jury should decide both the damages and whether the injunction should issue.
choices.rationale: The jury decides legal factual issues, but the judge determines whether to grant equitable relief.

explanation.testedIssue: How to sequence trial of common factual issues when a case combines legal damages and equitable relief.
explanation.governingRule: When legal and equitable claims share factual issues, the legal issues carrying a jury right generally must be tried to the jury before the court resolves the equitable claims.
explanation.application: The permission issue is common to the damages and injunction claims. The jury must determine it for the legal claim before the judge decides equitable relief.
explanation.commonTrap: Letting the presence of an injunction swallow the jury right on damages.
explanation.memoryAid: Common facts: jury on law first, judge on equity second.

authoring.contentWarning: Verify current law, wording, taxonomy, one-best-answer validity, and distractor quality before publication.

questions.subject: Civil Procedure
questions.subtopic: Renewed judgment as a matter of law - prerequisite pre-verdict motion
questions.stem: In a federal jury trial, the defendant believed that the plaintiff had introduced insufficient evidence of damages. The defendant did not move for judgment as a matter of law before the case was submitted to the jury. The jury returned a verdict for the plaintiff. Ten days after entry of judgment, the defendant filed a renewed motion for judgment as a matter of law based on the insufficiency of the damages evidence.
questions.call: May the court grant the renewed motion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because the motion was filed within 28 days after entry of judgment.
choices.rationale: Timeliness alone is insufficient; a renewed motion must rest on a proper pre-verdict motion.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because evidentiary sufficiency may always be raised after a jury verdict.
choices.rationale: Rule 50 requires a pre-verdict motion to preserve a renewed judgment-as-a-matter-of-law request.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because a court may never set aside a jury’s determination of damages.
choices.rationale: A court may grant judgment as a matter of law on damages when Rule 50 requirements are met.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, because the defendant did not make a motion for judgment as a matter of law before submission to the jury.
choices.rationale: A Rule 50(a) motion is a prerequisite to a Rule 50(b) renewed motion.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a party may obtain renewed judgment as a matter of law without first making a pre-verdict motion.
explanation.governingRule: A renewed motion under Rule 50(b) may be made only by a party that properly moved under Rule 50(a) before the case was submitted to the jury.
explanation.application: Although the post-verdict motion was timely, the defendant failed to preserve it by making the required pre-verdict motion.
explanation.commonTrap: Focusing on the 28-day deadline while overlooking preservation.
explanation.memoryAid: No Rule 50(a), no Rule 50(b).

authoring.contentWarning: Verify current law, wording, taxonomy, one-best-answer validity, and distractor quality before publication.

questions.subject: Civil Procedure
questions.subtopic: Claim preclusion - omitted damages from the same transaction
questions.stem: A bicyclist was struck by a delivery van. The bicyclist knew immediately that both his custom racing bicycle, worth $90,000, and his shoulder had been injured. He first sued the delivery company in federal court for the cost of replacing the bicycle. The applicable preclusion law follows the transactional approach. After a trial, the court entered judgment for the bicyclist. The bicyclist then filed a second action against the same company seeking damages for the shoulder injury arising from the same collision.
questions.call: Is the second action barred?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because both claims arose from the same transaction and the personal-injury claim could have been asserted in the first action.
choices.rationale: A valid final judgment bars later claims between the same parties arising from the same transaction that were or could have been litigated.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, because property damage and personal injury are separate causes of action for claim-preclusion purposes.
choices.rationale: Modern claim preclusion generally uses a transactional approach rather than separate forms of injury.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because the first court did not actually decide whether the bicyclist suffered a shoulder injury.
choices.rationale: Actual litigation of the precise issue is required for issue preclusion, not for claim preclusion.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, but only if the first judgment expressly stated that all claims from the collision were dismissed with prejudice.
choices.rationale: A judgment after trial is a final judgment on the merits without needing that additional statement.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a final judgment on one type of damage bars a later action for known damage from the same accident.
explanation.governingRule: Claim preclusion bars a later action between the same parties on claims arising from the same transaction or occurrence that were or could have been raised after a valid final judgment on the merits.
explanation.application: Both injuries were known and resulted from one collision. The bicyclist had to seek all available relief in the first action.
explanation.commonTrap: Using “actually litigated” language from issue preclusion.
explanation.memoryAid: One transaction, one lawsuit.

questions.subject: Civil Procedure
questions.subtopic: Interlocutory appeal - preliminary injunction
questions.stem: A federal district court entered an order granting a preliminary injunction that prohibited a company from enforcing a noncompetition agreement while litigation continued. No final judgment had been entered. The company filed a notice of appeal from the preliminary-injunction order.
questions.call: May the court of appeals hear the appeal immediately?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because only final judgments may be appealed to a federal court of appeals.
choices.rationale: Congress has authorized immediate appeals from specified interlocutory orders, including orders granting injunctions.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, unless the district court certifies that the order presents a controlling question of law.
choices.rationale: Certification under 28 U.S.C. § 1292(b) is unnecessary for an order granting a preliminary injunction.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, because an order granting a preliminary injunction is immediately appealable by statute.
choices.rationale: Section 1292(a)(1) permits an immediate appeal from an interlocutory order granting, denying, modifying, or dissolving an injunction.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, but only if the company first disobeys the injunction and is held in contempt.
choices.rationale: A party need not risk contempt because the injunction order itself is immediately appealable.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether an order granting a preliminary injunction may be appealed before final judgment.
explanation.governingRule: Federal courts of appeals have jurisdiction over interlocutory orders granting, continuing, modifying, refusing, or dissolving injunctions.
explanation.application: The order grants a preliminary injunction, placing it within an express statutory exception to the final-judgment rule.
explanation.commonTrap: Assuming that every interlocutory appeal requires discretionary certification.
explanation.memoryAid: Injunction orders get an immediate appeal.

authoring.contentWarning: Verify current law, wording, taxonomy, one-best-answer validity, and distractor quality before publication.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.subtopic: Standing - environmental and recreational injury
questions.stem: A federal environmental statute authorizes private suits against facilities that discharge pollutants in excess of their permits. An association sued a factory for ongoing unlawful discharges into a river. Protecting the river is germane to the association's mission, and the requested injunction requires no member to participate individually. One association member lives two miles downstream and formerly kayaked and watched birds along the river every week. Because of reasonable concerns about the discharges, she now avoids the river. An injunction would require the factory to comply with its permit.
questions.call: Does the association have Article III standing based on the member's circumstances?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because the member has suffered no physical injury or financial loss.
choices.rationale: Aesthetic and recreational injuries may be concrete injuries for standing purposes; physical or economic harm is not required.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because the member’s diminished use of the river is a concrete injury traceable to the discharges and redressable by an injunction.
choices.rationale: The member’s reasonable avoidance of a place she regularly used supplies injury in fact, causation, and redressability.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because only the federal government may sue to enforce an environmental permit.
choices.rationale: The question states that the statute authorizes private suits, so the asserted bar does not apply.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, because every member of the public has standing to challenge a violation of federal law.
choices.rationale: A generalized interest in lawful government is insufficient; the association must rely on a member’s concrete injury.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether reduced recreational and aesthetic use of a polluted river is a sufficient injury for Article III standing.
explanation.governingRule: Standing requires a concrete and particularized injury that is actual or imminent, fairly traceable to the defendant, and likely to be redressed by the requested relief. Recreational and aesthetic injuries can qualify.
explanation.application: The member regularly used the river, reasonably stopped because of the factory’s ongoing discharges, and would benefit from an order requiring compliance.
explanation.commonTrap: Assuming that standing requires bodily harm or monetary loss.
explanation.memoryAid: Use lost, defendant linked, relief helps.

authorityNotes: Friends of the Earth, Inc. v. Laidlaw Environmental Services

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.subtopic: Mootness - voluntary cessation
questions.stem: A theater sued a private licensing organization, alleging that the organization was enforcing an unconstitutional rule that barred certain political performances. After suit was filed, the organization suspended the rule but retained authority to reinstate it at any time and continued to defend the rule as lawful. The organization moved to dismiss the action as moot.
questions.call: Should the court grant the motion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because a defendant’s cessation of challenged conduct always ends a federal court’s jurisdiction.
choices.rationale: Voluntary cessation does not automatically moot a case because the defendant might resume the conduct.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because the theater is no longer being denied a license under the rule.
choices.rationale: The possibility of reinstatement remains live, and current nonenforcement does not by itself eliminate the controversy.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because a constitutional challenge can never become moot.
choices.rationale: Constitutional claims are subject to ordinary mootness principles.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, unless it is absolutely clear that the challenged conduct cannot reasonably be expected to recur.
choices.rationale: The organization retained the power to reinstate the rule and defended its legality, so it has not carried the heavy burden required for voluntary cessation.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a defendant’s temporary suspension of challenged conduct moots a request for prospective relief.
explanation.governingRule: Voluntary cessation moots a case only when the defendant shows that it is absolutely clear the allegedly wrongful conduct could not reasonably be expected to recur.
explanation.application: The organization may reinstate the rule at any time and has not disavowed it. The controversy therefore remains live.
explanation.commonTrap: Treating present nonenforcement as equivalent to permanent abandonment.
explanation.memoryAid: A defendant cannot moot a case by pausing and preserving restart power.

authorityNotes: Friends of the Earth, Inc. v. Laidlaw Environmental Services

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.subtopic: Congressional spending power - conditions on federal funds
questions.stem: Congress created a federal grant program that reimburses states for emergency-shelter construction. The statute clearly provides that a state accepting the funds must prohibit discrimination in shelters built with the grants. A state that refuses the condition loses only five percent of the new shelter funds and remains free to decline the program entirely. One state challenged the condition as beyond Congress's spending power.
questions.call: Is the condition constitutional?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because the condition is clear, relates to the funded program, serves the general welfare, and is not coercive.
choices.rationale: Congress may attach unambiguous, related conditions to federal funds so long as the financial pressure does not become compulsion and no independent constitutional bar applies.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, because Congress may never influence state policy through conditional grants.
choices.rationale: Conditional spending is a recognized congressional power when the applicable limits are satisfied.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because any threatened loss of federal funds is unconstitutionally coercive.
choices.rationale: Coercion requires much more than the modest loss described here; states remain genuinely free to decline.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, but only because Congress could directly require every state to operate emergency shelters.
choices.rationale: The condition may be valid under the spending power even if Congress could not directly commandeer state administration.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether Congress may condition a modest federal grant on a clear requirement related to the funded program.
explanation.governingRule: Spending conditions generally must promote the general welfare, be unambiguous, relate to the federal interest in the program, avoid independent constitutional violations, and not be so coercive that the state has no real choice.
explanation.application: The nondiscrimination condition is explicit and directly connected to federally funded shelters, and losing five percent of a new grant program is not coercive on these facts.
explanation.commonTrap: Equating all financial inducement with unconstitutional coercion.
explanation.memoryAid: Clear, related, lawful, and not a fiscal gun to the head.

authorityNotes: National Federation of Independent Business v. Sebelius

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.category: The relation of nation and states in a federal system
questions.subtopic: Anti-commandeering - orders to state legislatures
questions.stem: Congress enacted a federal statute requiring every state legislature to pass a law directing private recycling companies to submit monthly reports to a federal agency. The statute does not regulate the companies directly. A state refused to enact the required law and challenged the federal statute.
questions.call: Is the federal statute constitutional?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because regulating commercial recycling falls within Congress’s commerce power.
choices.rationale: Even when Congress may regulate private commerce directly, it generally may not require a state legislature to enact a federal regulatory program.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because federal law is supreme over conflicting state policy.
choices.rationale: The Supremacy Clause permits preemption of state law but does not authorize Congress to command states to legislate.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because Congress may not compel a state legislature to enact and administer a federal regulatory program.
choices.rationale: The statute directly orders state lawmakers to legislate, which violates the anti-commandeering principle.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, because Congress lacks power to regulate recycling companies engaged in interstate commerce.
choices.rationale: Congress may have commerce power over the companies; the defect is the means chosen to regulate them through compulsory state legislation.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether Congress may require state legislatures to enact laws carrying out a federal regulatory program.
explanation.governingRule: The federal government may regulate private actors directly and preempt contrary state law, but it generally may not command state legislatures or executive officials to enact or administer federal regulation.
explanation.application: The statute does not regulate recycling companies itself. It orders each state legislature to enact the federal policy, which is unconstitutional commandeering.
explanation.commonTrap: Assuming the Commerce Clause overrides structural federalism limits on the method of regulation.
explanation.memoryAid: Congress may regulate states under valid rules, but may not make them its legislature.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.subtopic: Appointments Clause - congressional appointment of executive officers
questions.stem: Congress created a five-member federal board with authority to investigate violations of federal consumer law, issue binding administrative orders, and bring enforcement actions in federal court. The statute directs the Speaker of the House to appoint three members and the president pro tempore of the Senate to appoint two members. The President has no appointment role. A company subject to a board order challenged the appointment method.
questions.call: Is the appointment method constitutional?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because Congress created the board and may therefore choose its members.
choices.rationale: Congress may establish offices but may not itself appoint officers who exercise significant federal authority except through the constitutionally prescribed actors.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, because officers exercising significant federal authority must be appointed in conformity with the Appointments Clause.
choices.rationale: The board members are federal officers, and legislative leaders are not authorized appointing authorities under the Clause.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, because the board performs both investigative and adjudicative functions rather than purely executive functions.
choices.rationale: The exercise of significant federal authority makes the members officers regardless of whether their duties span several functions.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, because every federal officer must be appointed personally by the President with Senate confirmation.
choices.rationale: Congress may vest appointment of inferior officers in the President alone, courts of law, or department heads; the stated rule is too broad.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether congressional leaders may appoint members of a federal board exercising significant governmental authority.
explanation.governingRule: Principal officers must be appointed by the President with Senate consent, and Congress may vest appointment of inferior officers only in the President, courts of law, or department heads. Congress itself may not appoint federal officers.
explanation.application: The board members issue binding orders and enforce federal law, making them officers. Appointment by legislative leaders violates the Appointments Clause.
explanation.commonTrap: Assuming that Congress’s power to create an agency includes power to staff it with officers of its choosing.
explanation.memoryAid: Congress creates offices; constitutional appointing actors fill them.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.subtopic: Equal protection - sex classifications
questions.stem: A state operates a publicly funded nursing school that admits only women. The state defends the policy by asserting that women have traditionally been the primary caregivers in families and are therefore generally better suited to nursing. A qualified male applicant who was denied admission sued under the Equal Protection Clause.
questions.call: How should the court evaluate the policy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Uphold it if the classification is rationally related to any legitimate state purpose.
choices.rationale: Government sex classifications receive heightened, not ordinary rational-basis, review.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Uphold it because education is not a fundamental constitutional right.
choices.rationale: The challenge concerns a sex classification, which independently triggers heightened scrutiny.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Invalidate it unless the state proves that the classification is necessary to achieve a compelling interest.
choices.rationale: Strict scrutiny is generally not the governing standard for sex classifications.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Invalidate it unless the state shows an exceedingly persuasive justification and a substantial relationship to an important objective.
choices.rationale: Sex classifications require an important governmental objective and means substantially related to that objective; stereotypes about caregiving are insufficient.

explanation.testedIssue: What level of review applies to a state school’s sex-based admissions restriction.
explanation.governingRule: Government sex classifications are subject to intermediate scrutiny and require an exceedingly persuasive justification: the classification must be substantially related to an important governmental objective and may not rest on overbroad stereotypes.
explanation.application: The state relies on a generalized stereotype about women’s roles rather than a demonstrated educational need. The policy is unlikely to survive intermediate scrutiny.
explanation.commonTrap: Looking only for a fundamental right and overlooking the sex classification.
explanation.memoryAid: Sex classification: important objective, substantial fit, no stereotypes.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.subtopic: Procedural due process - pretermination opportunity to respond
questions.stem: A city ordinance provides that permanent sanitation employees may be discharged only for cause. Without prior notice, the city fired a permanent employee for allegedly falsifying time records. The city offered the employee a full evidentiary hearing 30 days later but gave him no opportunity to respond before the termination took effect. The employee denied the accusation and sued.
questions.call: Did the city provide constitutionally sufficient process?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because the employee was ordinarily entitled to notice of the charge and a meaningful opportunity to respond before termination.
choices.rationale: A for-cause employment entitlement is property, and absent an emergency the government generally must provide a limited pretermination opportunity to respond, followed by fuller post-termination procedures.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because public employment is never property protected by due process.
choices.rationale: A for-cause limitation can create a legitimate entitlement to continued employment.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, because a full hearing within 30 days always cures the absence of any pretermination procedure.
choices.rationale: A later hearing does not ordinarily eliminate the requirement of basic notice and response before a nonemergency termination.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, because the Constitution requires a complete trial-type hearing before any public employee may be suspended or discharged.
choices.rationale: The pretermination hearing may be limited; due process does not always require a full evidentiary proceeding before discharge.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a permanent public employee with for-cause protection must receive a pretermination chance to respond.
explanation.governingRule: When government employment may be terminated only for cause, the employee generally has a property interest and must receive notice and a meaningful opportunity to respond before termination, with fuller procedures available afterward.
explanation.application: The city gave no advance notice or opportunity to respond and identified no emergency justifying immediate termination.
explanation.commonTrap: Assuming either that no pretermination process is needed or that a full trial is required immediately.
explanation.memoryAid: For-cause job: notice and response before the paycheck stops.

authorityNotes: Cleveland Board of Education v. Loudermill

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.subtopic: Takings - permanent physical occupation
questions.stem: A city ordinance requires every apartment owner to permit a private communications company to install a small metal relay box permanently on the roof of each building. The box occupies less than one square foot and does not reduce the building's market value. An owner objected and sought compensation under the Takings Clause.
questions.call: Is the owner entitled to just compensation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because the box causes no measurable decrease in market value.
choices.rationale: A permanent physical occupation is generally a per se taking even when the economic loss is slight.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, because the city itself does not own or operate the equipment.
choices.rationale: Government authorization of a permanent physical occupation by a private party can constitute a taking.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, because the ordinance authorizes a permanent physical occupation of private property.
choices.rationale: A compelled permanent physical occupation is a categorical taking requiring compensation.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, but only if the owner proves that the ordinance deprives the property of all economically beneficial use.
choices.rationale: Total economic deprivation is a separate categorical rule for regulations; it is not required for a permanent physical occupation.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a compelled, tiny permanent occupation by private equipment is a taking without proof of economic loss.
explanation.governingRule: A government-authorized permanent physical occupation of private property is generally a per se taking requiring just compensation, regardless of the area occupied or economic impact.
explanation.application: The ordinance permanently reserves roof space for another entity’s equipment. The small size and lack of market-value loss do not avoid compensation.
explanation.commonTrap: Applying the multi-factor regulatory-takings test to a permanent occupation.
explanation.memoryAid: Permanent physical occupation means compensation, even when small.

authorityNotes: Loretto v. Teleprompter Manhattan CATV Corp.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.subtopic: Free Exercise Clause - neutral and generally applicable law
questions.stem: A state requires every applicant for a driver's license to be photographed without any head covering. The rule contains no secular exceptions and applies to all applicants. A member of a religion that requires adherents to wear a head covering at all times sought an exemption. The state refused, explaining that unobstructed photographs aid identification. The applicant challenged the rule under the Free Exercise Clause.
questions.call: Is the applicant likely to prevail?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because the rule is neutral and generally applicable and only incidentally burdens religious practice.
choices.rationale: Under the federal Free Exercise Clause, a neutral and generally applicable law ordinarily need not satisfy strict scrutiny merely because it burdens religious conduct.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because every substantial burden on religious exercise triggers strict scrutiny.
choices.rationale: That is not the general federal constitutional rule for neutral, generally applicable laws.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, because religious conduct is absolutely immune from generally applicable regulation.
choices.rationale: Religious conduct may be subject to neutral, generally applicable laws.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, but only because operating a motor vehicle is a privilege rather than a right.
choices.rationale: The relevant issue is the neutrality and general applicability of the rule, not the right-privilege distinction.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a neutral rule with no exceptions must provide a religious exemption under the Free Exercise Clause.
explanation.governingRule: A neutral and generally applicable law that incidentally burdens religious conduct ordinarily is valid under the Free Exercise Clause without strict scrutiny, absent targeting or a system of discretionary exceptions.
explanation.application: The photograph rule applies to all applicants, contains no exceptions, and is justified without reference to religion.
explanation.commonTrap: Assuming that any religious burden automatically requires the government to grant an exemption.
explanation.memoryAid: Neutral plus generally applicable usually means no constitutional exemption.

authorityNotes: Church of the Lukumi Babalu Aye, Inc. v. City of Hialeah

authoring.contentWarning: Verify current law, wording, taxonomy, one-best-answer validity, and distractor quality before publication.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.subtopic: Freedom of expression - content-neutral parade permitting
questions.stem: A city requires a permit for every organized parade on its downtown streets. The ordinance requires applications at least seven days in advance, directs the permitting officer to approve an application unless the route conflicts with another event or creates a documented traffic hazard, requires a decision within two business days, and permits prompt judicial review. The rules apply without regard to a parade's message. The city also permits demonstrations on adjacent sidewalks without a parade permit and offers alternative routes when a scheduling conflict occurs. A political group challenged the permit requirement on its face.
questions.call: Is the requirement constitutional?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because streets are traditional public forums in which permits may never be required.
choices.rationale: Reasonable content-neutral permit systems may regulate the time, place, and manner of parades in traditional public forums.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, because requiring advance notice is always an unconstitutional prior restraint.
choices.rationale: Advance permit requirements are not automatically invalid when governed by objective standards and adequate procedural safeguards.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, because a city may deny any parade that it believes would be inconvenient.
choices.rationale: Unbounded discretion would be unconstitutional; this ordinance instead uses objective criteria.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, because it is a content-neutral time, place, and manner regulation with objective standards and prompt review.
choices.rationale: The ordinance serves traffic coordination, leaves speech channels open, limits official discretion, and provides timely decisions and review.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether an objective, content-neutral parade permit system in a traditional public forum is facially valid.
explanation.governingRule: The government may impose content-neutral time, place, and manner restrictions in a traditional public forum if they are narrowly tailored to a significant interest, leave ample alternatives, and do not vest unbridled discretion. Permit systems also require adequate procedural safeguards.
explanation.application: The ordinance uses message-neutral traffic criteria, fixed deadlines, and prompt review, making it a permissible coordination mechanism.
explanation.commonTrap: Treating every permit system as forbidden merely because streets are public forums.
explanation.memoryAid: Public forum permits need neutral rules, narrow discretion, and prompt decisions.

questions.subject: Contracts
questions.subtopic: Revocation - reliable indirect information
questions.stem: On Monday, an owner offered in writing to sell a parcel of land to a buyer for $300,000 and stated that the offer would remain open until Friday. The buyer gave no consideration for that promise. On Wednesday morning, a real estate broker who regularly represented the owner accurately told the buyer that the owner had sold the parcel to another person the previous evening. On Wednesday afternoon, before receiving any direct notice from the owner, the buyer delivered a signed acceptance to the owner.
questions.call: Was a contract formed between the owner and the buyer?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because the owner promised in writing to keep the offer open until Friday.
choices.rationale: Without consideration or another basis for an option, the promise to keep the offer open was revocable.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because revocation is effective only when communicated directly by the offeror.
choices.rationale: Reliable indirect information that the offeror has taken definite action inconsistent with the offer can terminate the power of acceptance.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because the buyer learned from a reliable source that the owner had taken definite action inconsistent with the offer.
choices.rationale: The completed sale, reliably communicated through the owner’s regular broker, indirectly revoked the offer before the attempted acceptance.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, because an offer to sell land automatically expires when the offeror begins negotiating with another buyer.
choices.rationale: Mere negotiation would not necessarily revoke the offer; the critical facts are the completed inconsistent sale and reliable notice.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether an offeree’s power of acceptance ends upon reliable indirect notice that the offeror sold the subject property.
explanation.governingRule: A revocable offer is terminated when the offeree receives reliable information that the offeror has taken definite action inconsistent with an intention to enter the proposed contract.
explanation.application: The buyer received accurate information from the owner’s regular broker that the land had already been sold. The buyer’s later acceptance was therefore ineffective.
explanation.commonTrap: Assuming that revocation must always come directly from the offeror.
explanation.memoryAid: Reliable news of a definite inconsistent act can revoke.

authoring.contentWarning: Verify current law, wording, taxonomy, one-best-answer validity, and distractor quality before publication.

questions.subject: Contracts
questions.subtopic: Option contracts - acceptance effective upon receipt
questions.stem: A seller gave a buyer a signed written option to purchase a warehouse at a stated price at any time before June 30. The buyer paid $1,000 for the option. On June 29, the buyer mailed an unconditional acceptance by a reasonable method. The seller received the acceptance on July 2. The option did not state when an acceptance would become effective.
questions.call: Did the buyer timely exercise the option?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because acceptance of an option contract is generally effective when received, not when dispatched.
choices.rationale: The mailbox rule ordinarily does not apply to the exercise of an option, so receipt after June 30 was untimely.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, because contracts involving land may never be accepted by mail.
choices.rationale: Mail may be a reasonable means of acceptance; the problem is the timing rule for options, not the subject matter.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, because every properly mailed acceptance is effective upon dispatch.
choices.rationale: The dispatch rule generally does not govern acceptance under an option contract.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, because the buyer paid consideration for the option.
choices.rationale: Consideration made the offer irrevocable but did not change the requirement that acceptance arrive before the option expired.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether the mailbox rule applies to an acceptance exercising a paid option.
explanation.governingRule: Unless the option provides otherwise, acceptance under an option contract is generally effective upon receipt by the offeror rather than upon dispatch.
explanation.application: The seller did not receive the acceptance until after the June 30 deadline, so the option was not timely exercised.
explanation.commonTrap: Applying the ordinary mailbox rule without recognizing the option-contract exception.
explanation.memoryAid: Options stay open, but acceptance must arrive on time.

authoring.contentWarning: Verify current law, wording, taxonomy, one-best-answer validity, and distractor quality before publication.

questions.subject: Contracts
questions.subtopic: Promissory estoppel - foreseeable reliance
questions.stem: A company offered a manager a position in another state, stating that the job would begin in six weeks and that the manager could rely on the offer. The company knew that accepting would require the manager to resign from a current job and move. The manager accepted, resigned, signed a one-year apartment lease near the new office, and paid moving expenses. Before the start date, the company withdrew the offer for business reasons unrelated to the manager. The contemplated employment could have been terminated by either party at any time after it began.
questions.call: Is the manager likely to recover at least reliance damages?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because at-will employment promises can never support any contractual remedy.
choices.rationale: Even when future employment would be at will, a clear pre-employment promise may support promissory-estoppel relief for foreseeable reliance.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, because the manager’s resignation and move were actions taken before beginning work.
choices.rationale: Those are precisely the types of foreseeable reliance that may make enforcement necessary to avoid injustice.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, and the manager is automatically entitled to salary for the entire anticipated career with the company.
choices.rationale: Promissory-estoppel relief is limited as justice requires and ordinarily does not guarantee speculative long-term expectation damages.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, because the company should reasonably have expected the promise to induce substantial reliance, and it did so.
choices.rationale: The company expressly invited reliance and knew relocation and resignation were necessary, supporting relief measured to avoid injustice.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether an at-will employment offer can support promissory-estoppel relief for pre-employment reliance.
explanation.governingRule: A promise reasonably expected to induce action or forbearance, which does induce such reliance, may be enforceable to the extent necessary to avoid injustice even without a bargained-for exchange.
explanation.application: The company knew and encouraged the manager to resign and relocate. Those substantial, foreseeable actions support at least reliance damages despite the at-will nature of the future job.
explanation.commonTrap: Treating at-will status as eliminating all liability for induced pre-employment reliance.
explanation.memoryAid: At will limits future tenure, not necessarily reliance already invited.

authoring.contentWarning: Verify current law, wording, taxonomy, one-best-answer validity, and distractor quality before publication.

questions.subject: Contracts
questions.subtopic: Unilateral mistake - knowledge by the other party
questions.stem: A contractor submitted a sealed bid of $410,000 to renovate a library. The contractor had intended to bid $510,000 but a clerical employee omitted $100,000 from the total. Before accepting, the library's purchasing officer compared the bid with four others ranging from $495,000 to $530,000 and noticed that one listed line item had not been included in the contractor's total. The officer immediately accepted the bid without asking about the discrepancy. The contractor discovered the error the next morning and sought rescission before work began.
questions.call: Is the contractor likely to obtain rescission?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because a unilateral mistake can never excuse contractual performance.
choices.rationale: A unilateral mistake may justify avoidance when the other party knew or had reason to know of it, among other limited circumstances.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because the library had reason to know of the material clerical error before accepting the bid.
choices.rationale: The unusually low bid and visible omission put the officer on notice, and prompt rescission before reliance is appropriate.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because the contractor bore the risk of every arithmetic error made by its employees.
choices.rationale: Although bidders normally bear calculation risks, avoidance may be available when the recipient knew or should have known of the mistake.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, because every mistake that makes a contract financially unfavorable permits rescission.
choices.rationale: A mere bad bargain is insufficient; the material mistake and the library’s notice are critical.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a contractor may avoid a bid based on a material clerical error apparent to the recipient.
explanation.governingRule: A contract may be voidable for unilateral mistake when enforcement would be unconscionable or the other party had reason to know of the mistake, provided the mistaken party did not bear the relevant risk and acts promptly.
explanation.application: The missing line item, large disparity, and comparable bids gave the library reason to know of the error. The contractor promptly sought rescission before performance.
explanation.commonTrap: Assuming that all unilateral mistakes are categorically borne by the mistaken party.
explanation.memoryAid: One-sided mistake plus the other side’s notice can undo the deal.

questions.subject: Contracts
questions.subtopic: Parol evidence - fully integrated agreement and prior contradictory term
questions.stem: A buyer and a seller negotiated the sale of a commercial generator. During negotiations, the seller orally promised that the generator would produce 500 kilowatts. The parties later signed a detailed agreement stating that the generator would produce 400 kilowatts and that the writing was the complete and exclusive statement of their agreement. After delivery, the generator produced 400 kilowatts. The buyer sued and offered evidence of the prior oral promise.
questions.call: Is the evidence admissible to establish the promised output?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because prior oral promises are always admissible to explain a written contract.
choices.rationale: Prior evidence may sometimes explain ambiguity, but it cannot ordinarily contradict an unambiguous term in a fully integrated agreement.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because the oral promise concerns the same subject as the writing.
choices.rationale: That fact makes the contradiction more direct rather than creating an exception to the parol evidence rule.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because the fully integrated writing expressly contradicts the prior oral promise.
choices.rationale: The writing states 400 kilowatts and contains a complete-integration clause, so the earlier 500-kilowatt promise cannot alter that term.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, because evidence of prior negotiations is inadmissible for every purpose.
choices.rationale: Such evidence may be admitted for purposes including fraud, mistake, interpretation of ambiguity, or showing no contract; the stated rule is too broad.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a prior oral term contradicting a complete written agreement is admissible to alter the agreement.
explanation.governingRule: When parties adopt a writing as a complete and final expression, evidence of prior or contemporaneous agreements may not be used to contradict the writing’s terms.
explanation.application: The writing expressly specifies 400 kilowatts and declares itself complete. The earlier promise of 500 kilowatts directly conflicts and is excluded for the offered purpose.
explanation.commonTrap: Mistaking the existence of negotiations for permission to rewrite a complete agreement.
explanation.memoryAid: Complete writing plus contradiction means exclusion.

questions.subject: Contracts
questions.subtopic: Substantial performance - construction contract
questions.stem: A builder agreed to construct a house for $600,000 using a specified brand of interior pipe. Acting in good faith, the builder installed a different brand of the same quality, appearance, and market value. Replacing the pipe would require substantial demolition and would cost $80,000, while the difference in the house's market value is $1,000. The builder otherwise completed the work and demanded the unpaid contract balance.
questions.call: What is the builder's likely recovery?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The contract balance minus the appropriate damages caused by the deviation.
choices.rationale: The builder substantially performed in good faith and may recover the price less damages, ordinarily measured here by the small diminution in value rather than economic waste.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Nothing, because strict compliance with every construction specification is a condition of payment as a matter of law.
choices.rationale: Constructive conditions in construction contracts generally require substantial, not perfect, performance absent a valid express condition.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The full contract balance with no deduction because the substitute pipe is functionally equivalent.
choices.rationale: The owner is still entitled to damages for the breach, even though the breach is minor and does not forfeit the builder’s recovery.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The contract balance minus the full $80,000 replacement cost in all circumstances.
choices.rationale: When replacement would cause gross economic waste and the deviation is incidental, diminution in value is the likely measure.

explanation.testedIssue: The effect of a good-faith, minor construction deviation on the contractor’s right to payment and damages.
explanation.governingRule: A contractor who substantially performs may recover the contract price minus damages for the defects. When repair would involve unreasonable economic waste, damages may be measured by diminution in value.
explanation.application: The house is complete, the substitute is equivalent, and replacement would be disproportionate. The builder can recover subject to a modest deduction.
explanation.commonTrap: Importing the UCC perfect-tender rule into a common-law construction contract.
explanation.memoryAid: Substantial performance earns the price minus the defect.

questions.subject: Contracts
questions.subtopic: Anticipatory repudiation - retraction
questions.stem: A manufacturer contracted to deliver custom equipment to a buyer on September 1. On July 1, the manufacturer unequivocally stated that it would not perform. On July 3, before the buyer canceled, obtained substitute equipment, materially changed position, or indicated that the repudiation was final, the manufacturer notified the buyer that it was retracting the repudiation and would perform on time. The buyer nevertheless treated the contract as terminated.
questions.call: Was the manufacturer's retraction effective?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because an anticipatory repudiation can never be withdrawn.
choices.rationale: A repudiating party may generally retract before the other party materially changes position or treats the repudiation as final.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, because the buyer acquired an irrevocable right to terminate immediately upon hearing the repudiation.
choices.rationale: The buyer’s rights remain subject to a timely retraction unless it cancels, materially relies, or otherwise signals finality.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, but only if the buyer expressly agrees to the retraction.
choices.rationale: The repudiating party may retract unilaterally under the stated circumstances.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, because the buyer had not materially relied on the repudiation or indicated that it considered the repudiation final.
choices.rationale: The retraction came before performance was due and before any event cutting off the right to retract.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether an anticipatory repudiation may be retracted before reliance, cancellation, or final acceptance of the repudiation.
explanation.governingRule: A repudiating party may retract before the next performance is due unless the aggrieved party has canceled, materially changed position in reliance, or otherwise indicated that the repudiation is considered final.
explanation.application: The manufacturer retracted promptly, and the buyer had taken none of the actions that would make the repudiation irrevocable.
explanation.commonTrap: Assuming that repudiation is permanently binding the instant it is spoken.
explanation.memoryAid: Repudiation can be pulled back until reliance or finality locks it in.

questions.subject: Contracts
questions.subtopic: Consequential damages - foreseeability at contract formation
questions.stem: A bakery hired a repair company to fix its only commercial oven by Monday. The bakery did not tell the company that it had accepted an unusually large, highly profitable catering order for Tuesday that could not be reassigned or completed without the oven. The company negligently finished the repair three days late. The bakery recovered the ordinary value of lost daily production and also sought the unusually large profit it would have earned on the special catering order.
questions.call: May the bakery recover the special catering profit?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because all losses factually caused by a breach are recoverable.
choices.rationale: Contract damages are limited by foreseeability, certainty, and mitigation; factual causation alone is insufficient.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, because the special loss was not reasonably foreseeable to the repair company when the contract was made.
choices.rationale: The company was not informed of the unusual order, so the exceptional profit was outside the ordinary consequences known at formation.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, because lost profits are always treated as direct damages.
choices.rationale: Lost profits may be consequential and must satisfy foreseeability and certainty requirements.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, because a business may never recover lost profits for breach of contract.
choices.rationale: Lost profits are recoverable when proved with reasonable certainty and within the parties’ contemplation; the stated rule is too broad.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a plaintiff may recover unusual consequential lost profits that were not disclosed at formation.
explanation.governingRule: Consequential damages are recoverable only if they were foreseeable as a probable result of breach when the contract was made, including special circumstances known to the breaching party.
explanation.application: The repair company could foresee ordinary production loss but had no reason to know about the exceptional catering contract. The special profit is not recoverable.
explanation.commonTrap: Treating every foreseeable-in-hindsight loss as compensable.
explanation.memoryAid: Special loss must be shared at formation.

questions.subject: Contracts
questions.subtopic: UCC battle of the forms - material additional term
questions.stem: A merchant buyer sent a signed purchase order to a merchant seller for 1,000 office chairs at a stated price. The order contained no arbitration provision and did not expressly limit acceptance to its terms. The seller promptly sent a signed acknowledgment accepting the order and adding a clause requiring arbitration of all disputes. The acknowledgment did not make acceptance expressly conditional on the buyer's assent to the arbitration clause. The buyer did not object, and the seller delivered the chairs. The jurisdiction treats an unexpected mandatory arbitration clause as a material alteration of this kind of commercial bargain.
questions.call: Is the arbitration clause part of the parties' contract?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because the buyer’s silence automatically accepted every additional term in the acknowledgment.
choices.rationale: Between merchants, additional terms may enter the contract, but material alterations do not become part merely through silence.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because the seller’s acknowledgment was the last form sent before performance.
choices.rationale: The common-law last-shot rule does not govern this UCC formation problem.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because the arbitration clause materially alters the bargain and was not expressly accepted by the buyer.
choices.rationale: The acknowledgment formed a contract, but a surprise or hardship-producing arbitration clause is ordinarily a material alteration excluded absent assent.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, because the different forms prevented any contract from arising until the chairs were delivered.
choices.rationale: A definite expression of acceptance can form a contract despite additional terms when acceptance is not expressly conditional.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a material additional term in a merchant’s acceptance becomes part of a UCC contract.
explanation.governingRule: A definite and timely expression of acceptance forms a contract despite additional terms unless acceptance is expressly conditional. Between merchants, an additional term becomes part unless the offer limits acceptance, the term materially alters the bargain, or timely objection occurs.
explanation.application: The acknowledgment was not conditional, so a contract formed. Arbitration materially alters the bargain and does not enter without express assent.
explanation.commonTrap: Applying either the common-law mirror-image rule or assuming every merchant term enters automatically.
explanation.memoryAid: UCC forms can make a contract while material extras stay out.

questions.subject: Contracts
questions.subtopic: UCC risk of loss - merchant seller
questions.stem: A consumer bought a refrigerator from an appliance store, a merchant. The contract required the consumer to pick up the identified refrigerator at the store on Saturday. The store completed all preparations for pickup and notified the consumer that the refrigerator was ready. On Friday night, before the consumer took possession, a lightning strike destroyed the store and the refrigerator without fault by either party. The contract contained no risk-of-loss term.
questions.call: Who bore the risk of loss when the refrigerator was destroyed?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The store, because a merchant seller bears the risk until the buyer receives the goods.
choices.rationale: When the seller is a merchant and there is no breach, risk ordinarily passes to the buyer only upon receipt of the goods.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The consumer, because the refrigerator had been identified to the contract.
choices.rationale: Identification is relevant but does not by itself transfer risk of loss from a merchant seller.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The consumer, because the store had notified the consumer that the refrigerator was ready.
choices.rationale: Tender and notice would be enough for a nonmerchant seller, but a merchant seller retains risk until receipt.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The store, but only because the consumer had not yet paid the full purchase price.
choices.rationale: Payment status is not the controlling UCC risk-of-loss rule on these facts.

explanation.testedIssue: When risk of loss passes from a merchant seller in a pickup transaction.
explanation.governingRule: Absent breach or contrary agreement, when the seller is a merchant, risk of loss passes to the buyer upon the buyer’s receipt of the goods.
explanation.application: The consumer had not yet taken physical possession, so the merchant store still bore the risk when the refrigerator was destroyed.
explanation.commonTrap: Using the tender-of-delivery rule that applies to nonmerchant sellers.
explanation.memoryAid: Merchant seller keeps risk until buyer receives.

questions.subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
questions.subtopic: Voluntary manslaughter - adequate provocation and no cooling time
questions.stem: A man unexpectedly came home and found his spouse engaged in sexual intercourse with another person. Immediately and while visibly distraught, the man seized a heavy lamp and struck the other person in the head, killing that person. The man had not suspected the affair and had no time to cool off before the killing. The jurisdiction follows the common law.
questions.call: Of what homicide offense is the man most likely guilty?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No homicide offense, because the discovery legally justified the use of deadly force.
choices.rationale: Provocation may mitigate murder but does not justify killing a person who is not posing an imminent threat.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Involuntary manslaughter, because the man did not plan the killing in advance.
choices.rationale: The man intentionally used deadly force; lack of advance planning does not make the killing involuntary.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Murder, because intent to kill can never be mitigated by provocation.
choices.rationale: At common law, adequate provocation combined with actual heat of passion and no cooling time can reduce an intentional killing to voluntary manslaughter.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Voluntary manslaughter, because the killing occurred in the heat of passion after adequate provocation and before a reasonable cooling period.
choices.rationale: The unexpected discovery is traditionally adequate provocation, and the immediate killing supports actual heat of passion without cooling.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether an intentional killing immediately following traditional adequate provocation is murder or voluntary manslaughter.
explanation.governingRule: An intentional killing is mitigated from murder to voluntary manslaughter when the defendant acts in actual heat of passion caused by legally adequate provocation, before a reasonable person would cool and before the defendant actually cools.
explanation.application: The man acted immediately after an unexpected discovery traditionally recognized as adequate provocation. The facts support mitigation but not complete justification.
explanation.commonTrap: Equating mitigation with acquittal, or assuming every intentional killing is murder.
explanation.memoryAid: Adequate provocation plus hot blood plus no cooling equals voluntary manslaughter.

authorityNotes: MBE assumption: common-law crimes unless stated otherwise
