questions.subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
questions.subtopic: Larceny by trick - fraudulent acquisition of possession
questions.stem: A collector asked an owner to lend him a rare watch for one afternoon so that he could show it to a potential buyer. At the time of the request, the collector intended to keep the watch permanently. Relying on the collector's lie, the owner voluntarily handed over the watch but intended to transfer possession only, not title. The collector immediately sold the watch and kept the proceeds. The jurisdiction follows the common law.
questions.call: What crime did the collector commit when he obtained the watch?

choices.label: A
choices.text: False pretenses, because the collector obtained the watch by a material misrepresentation.
choices.rationale: False pretenses requires obtaining title through fraud; the owner intended to transfer only temporary possession.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Larceny by trick, because the collector fraudulently obtained possession with a present intent to steal.
choices.rationale: Fraud induced the owner to transfer possession while retaining title, which is larceny by trick.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Embezzlement, because the collector later sold property entrusted to him.
choices.rationale: Embezzlement requires lawful possession at the outset; possession obtained by fraud with contemporaneous intent to steal is trespassory for larceny-by-trick purposes.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No theft offense until the collector sold the watch, because the owner voluntarily delivered it.
choices.rationale: Fraud vitiated the transfer of possession, and the collector’s intent existed when he obtained the watch.

explanation.testedIssue: Which common-law theft offense applies when fraud induces transfer of possession but not title.
explanation.governingRule: Larceny by trick occurs when a defendant, with intent to permanently deprive, obtains possession of property through fraud while the owner retains title.
explanation.application: The owner intended only a temporary loan, and the collector had a present intent to keep the watch. The offense was complete upon receipt.
explanation.commonTrap: Choosing false pretenses merely because a misrepresentation was used.
explanation.memoryAid: Fraud gets possession: larceny by trick. Fraud gets title: false pretenses.

questions.subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
questions.subtopic: Common-law burglary - breaking and entering with felonious intent
questions.stem: At midnight, a person went to a dwelling intending to steal jewelry. The person pushed open a closed but unlocked ground-floor window, climbed inside, and began searching a bedroom. The residents were away for the night. The jurisdiction defines burglary according to the common law.
questions.call: Has the person committed burglary?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because the residents were not physically present in the dwelling.
choices.rationale: A structure remains a dwelling for common-law burglary even when the occupants are temporarily absent.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, because opening an unlocked window is not a breaking.
choices.rationale: Using even slight force to open a closed entry point is an actual breaking.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, because the person broke and entered the dwelling of another at night with intent to commit a felony inside.
choices.rationale: The closed window was opened, entry occurred at night, and the person entered with larcenous intent.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, but only if the person actually removed jewelry from the dwelling.
choices.rationale: Burglary is complete upon the prohibited breaking and entering with the required intent; completion of the intended felony is unnecessary.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether pushing open an unlocked window and entering an temporarily unoccupied dwelling with intent to steal satisfies common-law burglary.
explanation.governingRule: Common-law burglary is the breaking and entering of the dwelling of another at night with intent to commit a felony therein. Opening a closed but unlocked window is a breaking.
explanation.application: The person opened a closed window and entered the dwelling at midnight intending larceny. The residents’ temporary absence does not matter.
explanation.commonTrap: Assuming that breaking requires damage or that the intended felony must be completed.
explanation.memoryAid: Closed entry opened plus nighttime dwelling plus felony intent equals burglary.

questions.subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
questions.subtopic: Attempt - factual impossibility
questions.stem: A pickpocket saw a wallet-shaped bulge in a commuter's back pocket and decided to steal the wallet. The pickpocket reached into the pocket and tried to remove the supposed wallet, but the pocket was empty; the bulge was caused by folded fabric. The jurisdiction follows the common law and requires an act dangerously close to completion for attempt.
questions.call: Is the pickpocket guilty of attempted larceny?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because the pickpocket intended larceny and came dangerously close to completing it; factual impossibility is no defense.
choices.rationale: The pickpocket had the requisite intent and performed the final conduct believed necessary to steal property.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, because larceny was factually impossible when the pocket contained no property.
choices.rationale: Factual impossibility does not excuse attempt when the crime would have occurred had circumstances been as the defendant believed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because reaching into a pocket is mere preparation as a matter of law.
choices.rationale: On these facts the pickpocket completed the physical act believed necessary, satisfying even the demanding proximity test.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, but only if the commuter actually owned a wallet elsewhere on his person.
choices.rationale: The existence of other property is irrelevant; attempt liability rests on intent and conduct toward the perceived target.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether attempting to steal from an empty pocket is punishable despite factual impossibility.
explanation.governingRule: Factual impossibility is not a defense to attempt. A defendant who has the specific intent to commit the crime and proceeds sufficiently close to completion is liable even when an unknown factual circumstance prevents success.
explanation.application: The pickpocket intended to steal and reached into the target pocket, completing the conduct believed necessary. The empty pocket is only a factual obstacle.
explanation.commonTrap: Treating inability to complete the offense under actual facts as a defense.
explanation.memoryAid: Facts make it impossible; attempt still possible.

questions.subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
questions.subtopic: Solicitation - completion and nonmerger when request is refused
questions.stem: A business owner asked an employee to burn a competitor's warehouse and offered to pay the employee $20,000. The employee immediately refused and reported the request to police. The owner took no further action. The jurisdiction follows the common law.
questions.call: Of what inchoate crime is the owner guilty?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No crime, because the employee refused to participate.
choices.rationale: Solicitation is complete when the request is made with the intent that the solicited offense be committed.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Attempted arson, because the owner intended that the warehouse be burned.
choices.rationale: The request alone is ordinarily not sufficiently proximate to the physical commission of arson to constitute attempt.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Conspiracy to commit arson, because the owner offered payment for the crime.
choices.rationale: Conspiracy requires an agreement; the employee refused, so no bilateral common-law agreement arose.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Solicitation, because the owner asked another person to commit arson with the intent that the crime be committed.
choices.rationale: The crime was complete upon the request, regardless of the employee’s refusal.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a rejected request to commit a felony constitutes solicitation, attempt, or conspiracy.
explanation.governingRule: Solicitation consists of asking, encouraging, or commanding another to commit a crime with the intent that the crime be committed. The solicited person need not agree.
explanation.application: The owner intentionally requested arson. Refusal prevents conspiracy but does not undo the completed solicitation.
explanation.commonTrap: Assuming that inchoate liability always requires agreement or an act near the target crime.
explanation.memoryAid: The request completes solicitation; acceptance is unnecessary.

questions.subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
questions.subtopic: Terry stop and frisk - reasonable suspicion of crime and danger
questions.stem: At 2:00 a.m., an experienced officer saw a man repeatedly walk past a closed jewelry store, peer through its windows, confer with another person in a parked car, and return to the store. The man continually touched a large bulge at his waistband. Believing that the two were preparing a robbery and that the man might be armed, the officer briefly stopped him and patted the outer clothing. The officer felt and removed a handgun.
questions.call: Was the stop and frisk constitutional?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because the officer lacked probable cause to arrest the man before the pat-down.
choices.rationale: A brief investigatory stop and protective frisk require reasonable suspicion, not probable cause to arrest.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because specific observations supported reasonable suspicion of criminal activity and that the man was armed and dangerous.
choices.rationale: The repeated surveillance, late hour, coordination, and waistband bulge justified both the stop and a limited weapons frisk.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because touching a waistband can never support a weapons frisk.
choices.rationale: The bulge and repeated touching are relevant when combined with the suspected robbery and other observations.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, because police may frisk every person they lawfully question on a public street.
choices.rationale: A frisk additionally requires reasonable suspicion that the person is armed and dangerous.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether observed conduct supplied reasonable suspicion for both an investigatory stop and a protective frisk.
explanation.governingRule: An officer may briefly stop a person based on reasonable suspicion of criminal activity and may frisk outer clothing when there is reasonable suspicion that the person is armed and dangerous.
explanation.application: The officer observed coordinated casing behavior and a suspicious waistband bulge in circumstances suggesting an imminent robbery. Those facts justify the limited intrusion.
explanation.commonTrap: Demanding probable cause for a Terry stop or treating a lawful stop as automatic authority to frisk.
explanation.memoryAid: Stop needs crime suspicion; frisk needs weapon danger.

questions.subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
questions.subtopic: Automobile exception - scope of search
questions.stem: Police lawfully stopped a car and developed probable cause to believe that stolen laptop computers were hidden somewhere inside it. Without obtaining a warrant, officers opened the trunk and then opened a closed duffel bag large enough to contain a laptop. They found stolen computers in the bag. The car was readily mobile.
questions.call: Was the search constitutional?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because police may search a car without a warrant only after arresting an occupant.
choices.rationale: The automobile exception is based on probable cause and does not require an arrest.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, because the automobile exception never permits opening closed containers.
choices.rationale: When probable cause extends to the vehicle generally, officers may search containers capable of holding the object sought.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, because probable cause to search the car permitted a search of areas and containers where laptops could be hidden.
choices.rationale: The trunk and duffel bag were within the permissible scope of the automobile exception.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, because every traffic stop permits a complete search of the stopped vehicle.
choices.rationale: A traffic stop alone is insufficient; the search was valid because the officers had probable cause regarding stolen laptops.

explanation.testedIssue: The permissible scope of a warrantless vehicle search based on probable cause.
explanation.governingRule: Under the automobile exception, police with probable cause may search the vehicle and any containers within it capable of concealing the object of the search.
explanation.application: Laptops could fit in the trunk and duffel bag, and probable cause concerned the vehicle as a whole. The search stayed within that scope.
explanation.commonTrap: Assuming containers receive greater protection than the vehicle when they can hold the evidence sought.
explanation.memoryAid: Probable cause defines where in the car police may look.

questions.subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
questions.subtopic: Miranda - public safety exception
questions.stem: Police arrested a suspected armed robber in a crowded supermarket. The suspect wore an empty shoulder holster, and officers had not found the gun. Before giving Miranda warnings, an officer asked, “Where is the gun?” The suspect replied that it was under a display near the checkout line. Police immediately recovered a loaded gun from that location. The prosecution seeks to use the statement at trial.
questions.call: Is the statement admissible?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because the question was reasonably prompted by an immediate concern for public safety.
choices.rationale: The missing loaded gun in a crowded store created an objectively reasonable need to secure the weapon before warnings.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, because every custodial question asked before Miranda warnings is inadmissible.
choices.rationale: The public-safety exception permits limited unwarned questioning reasonably necessary to address an immediate danger.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because the officer’s question was likely to produce an incriminating response.
choices.rationale: That makes it interrogation, but the public-safety exception can still render the response admissible.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, because physical evidence derived from an unwarned statement is always admissible, even if the statement itself is not.
choices.rationale: The question asks about the statement; its admissibility rests on the public-safety exception, not solely on treatment of derivative physical evidence.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether an unwarned custodial response identifying a missing gun is admissible under the public-safety exception.
explanation.governingRule: Police may ask questions reasonably prompted by an immediate concern for public safety before administering Miranda warnings, and the resulting answers may be admitted.
explanation.application: A loaded gun was missing in a crowded public place. Asking its location was directed at neutralizing the danger rather than conducting routine evidence gathering.
explanation.commonTrap: Applying Miranda mechanically without considering the immediate danger.
explanation.memoryAid: Secure the public danger first; warn before ordinary interrogation.

questions.subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
questions.subtopic: Sixth Amendment right to counsel - postindictment lineup
questions.stem: After a defendant was indicted for robbery and retained counsel, police placed the defendant in a live lineup without notifying counsel. A witness identified the defendant during the lineup. The defendant had not waived the right to counsel. At trial, the prosecution seeks to introduce evidence that the witness selected the defendant at the lineup.
questions.call: Should the court admit the lineup identification?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because the privilege against self-incrimination does not protect physical appearance.
choices.rationale: Although the Fifth Amendment does not bar lineups, the postindictment lineup implicates the Sixth Amendment right to counsel.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because counsel is required only during courtroom proceedings.
choices.rationale: A postcharge live lineup is a critical stage at which the Sixth Amendment right to counsel applies.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because every identification obtained without counsel is permanently inadmissible, including any later in-court identification.
choices.rationale: The lineup identification is suppressible, but a later in-court identification may be admitted if it has an independent source.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, because the postindictment lineup was a critical stage conducted without counsel or a valid waiver.
choices.rationale: The right to counsel had attached and applied to the live lineup, so evidence of that uncounseled identification should be excluded.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether evidence of a live lineup identification obtained after indictment without counsel is admissible.
explanation.governingRule: After formal adversarial proceedings begin, a live lineup is a critical stage at which the accused has a Sixth Amendment right to counsel unless that right is validly waived.
explanation.application: The defendant had been indicted, had counsel, and did not waive. The prosecution therefore may not use the lineup identification itself.
explanation.commonTrap: Analyzing only self-incrimination or assuming the exclusion automatically bars every future identification.
explanation.memoryAid: Postcharge live lineup means counsel at the lineup.

questions.subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
questions.subtopic: Double jeopardy - retrial after a deadlocked jury
questions.stem: A defendant was tried by a jury for robbery. After several days of deliberation, the jury informed the judge that it was hopelessly deadlocked. After questioning the foreperson and considering alternatives, the judge declared a mistrial over the defendant's objection. The prosecution then scheduled a new trial on the same charge.
questions.call: Does the Double Jeopardy Clause bar the new trial?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because jeopardy attached when the first jury was sworn.
choices.rationale: Jeopardy did attach, but retrial is still permitted after a mistrial supported by manifest necessity.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, because a genuinely deadlocked jury creates manifest necessity for a mistrial and permits retrial.
choices.rationale: A hung jury is the classic circumstance in which the public interest in a verdict permits a second trial.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, because the defendant did not consent to the mistrial.
choices.rationale: Defendant consent is not required when manifest necessity justifies the mistrial.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, because double jeopardy protections apply only after a conviction or acquittal.
choices.rationale: Double jeopardy applies once a jury is sworn, although particular exceptions permit retrial.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether retrial is permitted after a judge declares a mistrial because the jury is genuinely deadlocked.
explanation.governingRule: Once jeopardy attaches, retrial after a mistrial over the defendant’s objection is permitted when manifest necessity exists. A genuinely hung jury ordinarily satisfies that standard.
explanation.application: The judge reasonably determined that continued deliberation would not produce a verdict. The state may retry the charge.
explanation.commonTrap: Assuming that attachment of jeopardy makes every subsequent retrial impossible.
explanation.memoryAid: Hung jury means manifest necessity and a permissible retrial.

questions.subject: Evidence
questions.subtopic: Refreshing recollection - use of a writing
questions.stem: At a civil trial, a witness testified that she could not remember the license-plate number she saw immediately after an accident. Counsel showed her a note that she had written that evening. After silently reviewing the note, the witness stated that her memory was refreshed and testified to the plate number from her present recollection. Counsel did not offer the note into evidence. The opposing party objected to the testimony.
questions.call: How should the court rule?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Exclude the testimony, because the note itself is the best evidence of the plate number.
choices.rationale: The witness is testifying from refreshed present recollection, not proving the contents of the note, so the original-writing rule does not require exclusion.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Admit the testimony, because a writing may be used to refresh memory and the witness then testified from present recollection.
choices.rationale: A witness may consult a writing or other item to refresh memory; the testimony comes from the witness’s revived recollection.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Exclude the testimony, because only a document admitted as a recorded recollection may refresh a witness’s memory.
choices.rationale: Refreshing recollection and the recorded-recollection hearsay exception are distinct procedures.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Admit the testimony only if counsel first proves that the note was made in the regular course of business.
choices.rationale: Business-record requirements do not govern use of a writing merely to refresh recollection.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a witness may testify after consulting an unadmitted writing that refreshes present memory.
explanation.governingRule: A witness may use almost any item to refresh recollection. Once refreshed, the witness testifies from present memory. The adverse party may inspect the writing and use it on cross-examination.
explanation.application: The witness reviewed her own note, regained present recollection, and testified. The note need not itself satisfy a hearsay exception or be admitted by the proponent.
explanation.commonTrap: Confusing refreshing recollection with admitting a recorded recollection.
explanation.memoryAid: Refresh: witness speaks. Recorded recollection: record speaks.

questions.subject: Evidence
questions.subtopic: Impeachment - conviction requiring a dishonest act or false statement
questions.stem: A witness in a federal civil trial was convicted four years ago of filing a fraudulent tax return, an offense whose statutory elements require a knowingly false statement. The witness completed the sentence three years ago. The opposing party seeks to impeach the witness with the conviction. The witness argues that the court should exclude it because its prejudicial effect exceeds its probative value.
questions.call: How should the court rule?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Exclude the conviction, because convictions may be used only in criminal cases.
choices.rationale: Rule 609 applies in both civil and criminal cases.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Exclude the conviction unless the crime was punishable by more than one year in prison.
choices.rationale: For crimes requiring a dishonest act or false statement, admissibility does not depend on felony punishment.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Admit the conviction only if the court first finds that its probative value substantially outweighs its prejudicial effect.
choices.rationale: The special balancing test applies to certain older convictions or criminal defendants; qualifying dishonesty convictions within the time limit are admitted under Rule 609(a)(2).

choices.label: D
choices.text: Admit the conviction, because the offense required proof of a dishonest act or false statement and is within the time limit.
choices.rationale: A recent conviction whose elements require deceit or false statement must be admitted for impeachment.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a recent conviction for an offense requiring a knowingly false statement is admissible to impeach a witness.
explanation.governingRule: For any witness, evidence of a conviction must be admitted if establishing the elements of the crime required proving a dishonest act or false statement, subject to the ten-year limit and related provisions.
explanation.application: The tax offense necessarily involved a knowingly false statement, and the conviction is recent. It is admissible for impeachment.
explanation.commonTrap: Applying the felony balancing rules to a crime that falls under the dishonesty provision.
explanation.memoryAid: Element of deceit means Rule 609(a)(2).

questions.subject: Evidence
questions.category: Relevancy and reasons for excluding relevant evidence
questions.subtopic: Habit evidence - regular response to a repeated situation
questions.stem: A driver sued another motorist after an intersection collision. A disputed issue is whether the driver fastened her seat belt before leaving home that morning. The driver's spouse will testify that for the past ten years the driver has automatically fastened her seat belt immediately upon sitting in any car, without exception, even for trips across a parking lot. The spouse did not see the driver on the morning of the collision.
questions.call: Is the spouse's testimony admissible?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, as evidence of the driver’s habit when entering a car.
choices.rationale: The testimony describes a specific, regular, virtually automatic response to a repeated situation and is admissible to show conduct in conformity on the occasion.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, because character evidence may not be used to prove conduct in a civil case.
choices.rationale: Habit is distinct from general character and may be admitted to prove conduct on a particular occasion.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because the spouse lacks personal knowledge of the morning of the collision.
choices.rationale: A habit witness need not observe the event in question; personal knowledge of the repeated conduct supplies the foundation.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, but only if the driver also testifies that she remembers fastening the belt that morning.
choices.rationale: Habit evidence is valuable precisely when direct recollection or observation is unavailable.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether evidence of an invariable seat-belt routine is admissible to prove conduct on a particular occasion.
explanation.governingRule: Evidence of a person’s habit or an organization’s routine practice may be admitted to prove that the person or organization acted in accordance with the habit or practice on a particular occasion.
explanation.application: The spouse describes a highly specific and automatic response repeated for years, not a broad personality trait.
explanation.commonTrap: Labeling every repeated behavior as impermissible character evidence.
explanation.memoryAid: Habit is specific, repeated, and automatic.

questions.subject: Evidence
questions.category: Relevancy and reasons for excluding relevant evidence
questions.subtopic: Other-acts evidence - identity through distinctive method
questions.stem: A defendant is charged with robbing a jewelry store while wearing a silver theater mask and leaving behind a playing card marked with a hand-drawn black star. The prosecution offers evidence that two weeks earlier the defendant robbed another jewelry store in the same city while wearing an identical silver theater mask and left the same unusually marked playing card. The defendant disputes identity. The prosecution gave timely notice and offers the earlier robbery only to prove identity.
questions.call: Is the earlier robbery admissible?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because evidence of any other crime is inadmissible to prove anything in a criminal case.
choices.rationale: Other acts may be admitted for proper nonpropensity purposes, including identity.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, because identity can be proved only by eyewitness testimony or scientific evidence.
choices.rationale: Distinctive modus-operandi evidence may be highly probative of identity.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, if its probative value is not substantially outweighed by unfair prejudice, because the highly distinctive method tends to prove identity.
choices.rationale: The unusual combination of mask and marked card functions like a signature and is offered for a proper purpose.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, because a prior robbery proves that the defendant has a criminal character and therefore probably committed this robbery.
choices.rationale: Using the act solely for propensity would be impermissible; its admissibility depends on the nonpropensity identity theory.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a highly distinctive prior act is admissible to prove identity rather than criminal propensity.
explanation.governingRule: Evidence of another act is inadmissible solely to prove propensity but may be admitted for another purpose, such as identity, when the acts share sufficiently distinctive features and Rule 403 is satisfied.
explanation.application: The rare combination of an identical mask and uniquely marked card makes the earlier robbery strongly probative of the robber’s identity.
explanation.commonTrap: Choosing the right result for the forbidden reason that bad people repeat bad acts.
explanation.memoryAid: Signature method may prove identity; propensity may not.

questions.subject: Evidence
questions.category: Relevancy and reasons for excluding relevant evidence
questions.subtopic: Expert testimony - reliance on otherwise inadmissible information
questions.stem: In a federal products-liability trial, a qualified engineering expert formed an opinion after reviewing the damaged machine, published testing data, and reports prepared by technicians who assisted in the laboratory. Some technician reports would be inadmissible hearsay if offered for their truth, but experts in the field reasonably rely on such reports when evaluating machine failures. The expert proposes to state the resulting opinion without disclosing the inadmissible details to the jury.
questions.call: May the expert base the opinion on the technician reports?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because every fact supporting an expert opinion must itself be independently admissible.
choices.rationale: Experts may rely on inadmissible facts or data if experts in the field would reasonably rely on them.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because experts in the field reasonably rely on such reports, even though the reports are not independently admissible.
choices.rationale: Rule 703 permits the basis; limiting disclosure of the inadmissible details avoids using the expert as a conduit for hearsay.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because an expert may rely only on personal observations made during the litigation.
choices.rationale: Experts routinely rely on information supplied by others and learned outside court.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, and the reports must automatically be admitted as substantive evidence along with the opinion.
choices.rationale: Permitting reliance does not make the underlying inadmissible material automatically admissible to the jury.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether an expert may rely on inadmissible technician reports customarily used by experts in the field.
explanation.governingRule: An expert may base an opinion on facts or data that are not independently admissible if experts in the particular field would reasonably rely on those kinds of facts or data. Disclosure to the jury is separately limited.
explanation.application: The technician reports are the type reasonably relied upon by engineers, and the expert will not recite their inadmissible contents.
explanation.commonTrap: Assuming that expert reliance automatically transforms hearsay into substantive evidence.
explanation.memoryAid: Reasonable expert reliance permits the basis, not automatic admission of the basis.

questions.subject: Evidence
questions.subtopic: Compromise negotiations - statements and offers
questions.stem: After a delivery truck struck a storefront, the store owner demanded $80,000 from the trucking company. The company disputed both negligence and the amount of damage. During a meeting to settle the disputed claim, the company's manager said, “Our driver was looking at his phone, but your repair estimate is far too high. We will pay $30,000 to end this.” At trial, the owner offers both the statement about the phone and the $30,000 offer to prove liability.
questions.call: Are the statement and offer admissible for that purpose?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because factual admissions made during settlement discussions are admissible even when the offer itself is excluded.
choices.rationale: Under the Federal Rules, statements made during compromise negotiations are generally excluded along with the offer when used to prove liability or amount.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because the company disputed only the amount of damages and not the validity of the claim.
choices.rationale: The facts state that the company disputed both negligence and amount, so a disputed claim existed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The statement is admissible but the offer is inadmissible.
choices.rationale: Rule 408 generally excludes both compromise offers and accompanying negotiation statements for the prohibited purpose.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Neither is admissible to prove liability, because both were made during compromise negotiations over a disputed claim.
choices.rationale: The owner offers the material for the precise purpose Rule 408 forbids.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a factual admission accompanying a settlement offer is admissible to prove liability.
explanation.governingRule: Offers to compromise a disputed claim and statements made during compromise negotiations are generally inadmissible when offered to prove or disprove the validity or amount of that claim.
explanation.application: Negligence and damages were disputed, and the manager made both statements in an effort to settle. Neither may be used to prove liability.
explanation.commonTrap: Applying the old distinction that excluded the offer but admitted associated factual statements.
explanation.memoryAid: Disputed claim plus settlement talk means exclude offer and negotiation statements.

questions.subject: Evidence
questions.subtopic: Attorney-client privilege - voluntary disclosure to a third party
questions.stem: A client privately emailed an attorney seeking legal advice about a potential lawsuit. The attorney replied with legal analysis. The client then forwarded the attorney's full reply to a friend who had no role in obtaining or providing legal services. During later litigation, the opposing party learned of the forwarding and sought the communication. The client asserted attorney-client privilege.
questions.call: Is the communication still privileged?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because a communication that begins as privileged remains privileged regardless of later disclosure.
choices.rationale: Voluntary disclosure to an unnecessary third party generally waives confidentiality and privilege as to the communication.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because the friend promised not to share the email with anyone else.
choices.rationale: A confidentiality promise from an unrelated friend does not preserve the legal privilege after voluntary disclosure.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because the client voluntarily disclosed the privileged legal communication to an unnecessary third party.
choices.rationale: Forwarding the attorney’s advice to a friend ordinarily waives privilege for that communication.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, because emails can never qualify for attorney-client privilege.
choices.rationale: Electronic communications can be privileged when made confidentially for legal advice.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether forwarding privileged legal advice to an unrelated friend waives attorney-client privilege.
explanation.governingRule: Attorney-client privilege protects confidential communications for legal advice, but voluntary disclosure to a third party outside the privileged relationship generally waives the privilege.
explanation.application: The friend was unnecessary to the legal consultation, and the client voluntarily sent the complete legal advice to that friend.
explanation.commonTrap: Assuming that original privilege status survives every later disclosure.
explanation.memoryAid: Privilege needs continuing confidentiality.

questions.subject: Evidence
questions.subtopic: Original-writing rule - witness with personal knowledge of an event
questions.stem: A security camera recorded a collision in a parking lot. A bystander personally watched the collision from ten feet away. At trial, the camera recording is unavailable, and the bystander is called to testify about what she saw. The opposing party objects that the original-writing rule requires production of the video.
questions.call: How should the court rule?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Overrule the objection, because the bystander may testify from personal knowledge of the event without producing the recording.
choices.rationale: The testimony proves the collision itself, not the contents of the video, so the original-writing rule does not apply.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Sustain the objection, because a recording is always the exclusive means of proving an event that it captured.
choices.rationale: A recording does not displace competent eyewitness testimony about the underlying event.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sustain the objection unless the bystander first proves that the video was destroyed in good faith.
choices.rationale: Loss of the recording would matter if a party sought to prove its contents; the bystander relies on independent perception.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Overrule the objection only if the bystander helped operate the camera.
choices.rationale: Operation of the camera is irrelevant to testimony based on direct observation of the collision.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether the original-writing rule bars eyewitness testimony when a missing video also recorded the event.
explanation.governingRule: The original-writing rule applies when a party seeks to prove the content of a writing, recording, or photograph. It does not bar testimony based on independent personal knowledge of the underlying event.
explanation.application: The bystander watched the collision directly and may describe it without proving what the video depicts.
explanation.commonTrap: Treating a recording as automatically superior and therefore legally mandatory.
explanation.memoryAid: Proving the event is not the same as proving the recording’s content.

questions.subject: Evidence
questions.category: Hearsay and circumstances of its admissibility
questions.subtopic: Statements for medical diagnosis or treatment
questions.stem: Immediately after falling from a ladder, an injured worker told an emergency-room physician, “I fell about twelve feet and landed directly on my left shoulder.” The physician asked the question to diagnose possible fractures and internal injuries. The worker later sued the ladder manufacturer and is unavailable at trial. The worker's statement appears in the physician's testimony.
questions.call: Is the statement admissible over a hearsay objection?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because statements describing how an injury occurred are never pertinent to medical diagnosis.
choices.rationale: General cause and mechanism of injury may be highly pertinent to diagnosis and treatment.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, because the worker made the statement in anticipation of possible litigation.
choices.rationale: The relevant inquiry is whether the statement was made for and reasonably pertinent to medical diagnosis or treatment; the emergency context strongly supports that purpose.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, but only if the physician wrote the statement in a regularly kept medical record.
choices.rationale: The statement may qualify independently under the medical-diagnosis exception without relying on the business-record exception.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, because the statement described the cause and symptoms for purposes reasonably pertinent to medical diagnosis or treatment.
choices.rationale: The height and manner of the fall would guide examination and treatment, satisfying the exception.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a patient’s description of the mechanism of injury to an emergency physician is admissible for medical diagnosis or treatment.
explanation.governingRule: A statement made for medical diagnosis or treatment and reasonably pertinent to that purpose is not excluded by the hearsay rule, including statements describing medical history, symptoms, or the general cause of the condition.
explanation.application: The physician needed to know the height and impact point to diagnose the worker’s injuries. The statement was medically pertinent.
explanation.commonTrap: Assuming the exception is limited to descriptions of present pain and excludes causation entirely.
explanation.memoryAid: Medical-purpose statements may include how the injury happened when diagnosis needs it.

questions.subject: Evidence
questions.category: Hearsay and circumstances of its admissibility
questions.subtopic: Confrontation Clause - statements during an ongoing emergency
questions.stem: During an ongoing domestic assault, a neighbor called 911 and said, while crying, “A man is hitting a woman in the apartment next door right now. I can hear her screaming, and I saw him holding a metal bar.” The caller gave the apartment number so officers could respond. The caller died before the assailant's criminal trial. The recording is offered as an excited utterance, and the defense objects under both the hearsay rule and the Confrontation Clause.
questions.call: Should the recording be admitted?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because every out-of-court statement to a government employee is testimonial.
choices.rationale: Statements made primarily to obtain help during an ongoing emergency are generally nontestimonial.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because the statement qualifies as an excited utterance and its primary purpose was to obtain help during an ongoing emergency.
choices.rationale: The caller described startling events while under stress, and the emergency-focused 911 call is nontestimonial for Confrontation Clause purposes.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because the caller is unavailable and the defendant had no prior opportunity to cross-examine the caller.
choices.rationale: That requirement applies to testimonial hearsay; this emergency call is nontestimonial.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, because the Confrontation Clause never applies to hearsay exceptions.
choices.rationale: Some statements fitting hearsay exceptions remain testimonial and require confrontation; the admissibility here depends on the call’s primary purpose.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether an emergency 911 statement is admissible as an excited utterance without prior cross-examination.
explanation.governingRule: An excited utterance is admissible when made under the stress of a startling event. The Confrontation Clause bars testimonial hearsay absent unavailability and prior cross-examination, but statements primarily aimed at resolving an ongoing emergency are generally nontestimonial.
explanation.application: The assault was happening during the call, the caller was distressed, and the information directed police to an immediate danger. Both hearsay and confrontation requirements are satisfied.
explanation.commonTrap: Assuming either that all 911 calls are testimonial or that every hearsay exception defeats confrontation.
explanation.memoryAid: Ask the primary purpose: emergency help is usually nontestimonial.

questions.subject: Real Property
questions.subtopic: Future interests - contingent remainder subject to a condition precedent
questions.stem: An owner conveyed land “to Ava for life, then to Ben if Ben reaches age 21 before Ava dies; otherwise to Carla.” At the time of the conveyance, Ben was 12 years old. No jurisdictional statute changes the common-law classification of future interests.
questions.call: What interest does Ben hold immediately after the conveyance?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A vested remainder, because Ben is an ascertained person.
choices.rationale: Although Ben is ascertained, his right to possession is subject to the unmet condition precedent of reaching age 21 before Ava dies.

choices.label: B
choices.text: An executory interest, because Ben’s interest may divest Ava’s life estate.
choices.rationale: Ben’s interest can become possessory only at the natural expiration of Ava’s life estate and does not cut that estate short.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A contingent remainder, because Ben must satisfy a condition precedent before becoming entitled to possession.
choices.rationale: Reaching age 21 before Ava dies is an express condition precedent, so the remainder is contingent.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No interest, because a future interest cannot be created in a person under age 18.
choices.rationale: Minors may hold future interests; age affects the stated condition, not capacity to receive the interest.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a remainder given to an ascertained child subject to an age condition before the life tenant’s death is vested or contingent.
explanation.governingRule: A remainder is contingent if it is given to an unascertained person or is subject to an express condition precedent other than the natural termination of the preceding estate.
explanation.application: Ben is identified, but he must reach 21 before Ava dies to become entitled to possession. That unmet condition makes his remainder contingent.
explanation.commonTrap: Assuming that being an ascertained taker is sufficient for vesting despite an express condition precedent.
explanation.memoryAid: Known person plus unmet condition precedent still means contingent remainder.

questions.subject: Real Property
questions.subtopic: Cotenancy - duty to account for rents received from third parties
questions.stem: Two siblings own an office building as tenants in common, each with a one-half interest. Without consulting the other, one sibling leased the entire building to a business for one year and collected $60,000 in rent. The lease did not exclude the nonleasing sibling from asserting ownership rights, and the nonleasing sibling did not agree to waive an accounting.
questions.call: What amount, if any, must the leasing sibling ordinarily account for to the other sibling?

choices.label: A
choices.text: One-half of the net rent actually received.
choices.rationale: A cotenant who collects rents from third parties must account to the other cotenants for their proportionate shares, after appropriate expenses.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Nothing, because each tenant in common may possess and use the entire property.
choices.rationale: A cotenant’s possession right does not permit retaining all third-party rents derived from jointly owned property.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The entire $60,000, because one cotenant may not enter any lease without unanimous consent.
choices.rationale: A cotenant may lease that cotenant’s interest and sometimes purport to lease the whole, subject to the other cotenant’s rights; the remedy is proportional accounting, not forfeiture of all rent.

choices.label: D
choices.text: One-half of the fair rental value, regardless of the rent actually received or expenses paid.
choices.rationale: The ordinary duty is to account for a proportionate share of net rents actually collected from third parties.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a cotenant who collects third-party rent must share it with another cotenant.
explanation.governingRule: A cotenant who receives rent or other income from third parties for use of commonly owned property must account to the other cotenants for their proportionate shares of net income.
explanation.application: The leasing sibling collected $60,000 from a third party and must share the net proceeds equally because the ownership interests are equal.
explanation.commonTrap: Confusing a cotenant’s right to occupy the whole property with a right to keep all income generated from it.
explanation.memoryAid: Possession may be shared; third-party rent must be accounted for.

questions.subject: Real Property
questions.subtopic: Landlord-tenant - implied warranty of habitability
questions.stem: A residential tenant's apartment lost heat during a severe winter. The tenant immediately gave the landlord written notice. Although the heating system could have been repaired within one day at modest cost, the landlord did nothing for three weeks. The apartment was unsafe to occupy during that period. The tenant remained in the apartment and withheld a reasonable portion of the rent attributable to the loss of habitability. The jurisdiction recognizes the modern implied warranty of habitability.
questions.call: Is the tenant likely justified in withholding the reasonable portion of rent?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because a tenant must continue paying full rent unless the lease expressly guarantees heat.
choices.rationale: The implied warranty of habitability supplies minimum residential standards even without an express lease term.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, because withholding rent is available only after the tenant permanently vacates the premises.
choices.rationale: Modern habitability remedies may include rent withholding or abatement while the tenant remains, subject to jurisdictional procedures.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, but only if the landlord intentionally caused the heating failure.
choices.rationale: A landlord may breach by failing to repair after notice; intentional creation of the defect is unnecessary.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, because the landlord failed after notice to correct a serious condition affecting basic habitability.
choices.rationale: Reliable heat in severe winter is a basic residential requirement, and the landlord’s prolonged failure supports a reasonable rent abatement.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a residential tenant may withhold a reasonable amount of rent after notice of a serious habitability defect.
explanation.governingRule: Residential leases generally include an implied warranty that the premises are fit for basic human habitation. After notice and a reasonable opportunity to repair, breach may support remedies such as rent abatement or withholding, subject to applicable procedure.
explanation.application: The lack of heat made the apartment unsafe, the landlord had notice, and repair was feasible. A proportionate withholding is justified under the stated modern rule.
explanation.commonTrap: Importing constructive-eviction requirements, such as vacating, into an implied-warranty claim.
explanation.memoryAid: Habitability can reduce rent even when the tenant stays.

authorityNotes: Modern implied warranty of habitability doctrine

questions.subject: Real Property
questions.subtopic: Prescriptive easement - exclusivity not required
questions.stem: For 15 years, a homeowner openly and continuously used a visible path across a neighbor's land to reach a public road. The homeowner never asked permission, and the neighbor knew of the use but did not object. Other nearby residents occasionally used the same path. The statutory period for prescription is 10 years.
questions.call: Has the homeowner acquired a prescriptive easement?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because use must be exclusive in the same way possession must be exclusive for adverse possession.
choices.rationale: A claimant seeking a prescriptive easement generally need not exclude all other users; the use must be adverse, open, and continuous for the period.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because the homeowner’s open, adverse, and continuous use continued for longer than the statutory period.
choices.rationale: The known, nonpermissive use for 15 years satisfies the stated elements, and occasional use by others does not defeat the easement.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because an easement can be created only by a signed writing.
choices.rationale: Prescription is a nonwritten method of acquiring an easement through qualifying use over time.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, but only because the neighbor personally observed every use of the path.
choices.rationale: Open and notorious use must provide notice, but actual observation of every instance is unnecessary.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether occasional use by others prevents acquisition of a prescriptive easement.
explanation.governingRule: A prescriptive easement generally arises from open and notorious, adverse, and continuous use for the statutory period. Unlike adverse possession of title, strict exclusivity is usually not required.
explanation.application: The homeowner used the visible route without permission for 15 years with the neighbor’s knowledge. Shared occasional use does not defeat the claim.
explanation.commonTrap: Automatically importing every adverse-possession element into prescription.
explanation.memoryAid: Prescription needs adverse use over time, not sole use of the path.

questions.subject: Real Property
questions.subtopic: Equitable servitudes - enforcement against a successor with notice
questions.stem: An owner of two adjoining lots sold Lot 1 by a deed stating that Lot 1 could be used only for residential purposes and that the restriction was intended to benefit Lot 2 and bind successors. The deed was properly recorded. The buyer of Lot 1 later conveyed it to a successor who had record notice of the restriction. The successor began constructing a retail store. The current owner of Lot 2 seeks an injunction. Assume the restriction is otherwise lawful.
questions.call: Is the owner of Lot 2 likely to obtain the injunction?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because horizontal privity is always required to enforce a land restriction in equity.
choices.rationale: Horizontal privity is not required for the burden of an equitable servitude.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, because a successor can be bound only by restrictions stated in the successor’s own deed.
choices.rationale: A recorded restriction in the chain of title can give notice and bind a successor even if not repeated verbatim in the later deed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, because the written restriction was intended to run, touches and concerns the land, and the successor had notice.
choices.rationale: Those facts satisfy the traditional requirements for enforcing the burden of an equitable servitude by injunction.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, but only if the owner of Lot 2 proves monetary damages with certainty.
choices.rationale: An injunction is the characteristic equitable remedy, and proof of calculable monetary loss is not a prerequisite on these facts.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a recorded residential-use restriction may be enforced by injunction against a successor with notice.
explanation.governingRule: The burden of an equitable servitude generally binds a successor when the covenant is in writing, the original parties intended succession, it touches and concerns the land, and the successor has notice. Horizontal privity is not required.
explanation.application: The deed expressly created a residential restriction for Lot 2’s benefit, indicated intent to bind successors, and was recorded before the successor acquired Lot 1.
explanation.commonTrap: Demanding the privity rules associated with damages on a real covenant when the plaintiff seeks equitable enforcement.
explanation.memoryAid: Equity asks writing, intent, land relation, and notice.

questions.subject: Real Property
questions.subtopic: Marketable title - existing zoning violation
questions.stem: A seller contracted to convey a commercial building with marketable title. Before closing, the buyer discovered that an enclosed rear addition had been built in violation of a zoning setback ordinance. The city had issued a written notice requiring removal of the addition, and the violation had not been cured. The contract did not require the buyer to accept the violation.
questions.call: May the buyer refuse to close on the scheduled date?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because the existing zoning violation and removal order expose the buyer to litigation and impair marketable title.
choices.rationale: A current governmental violation affecting the property can render title unmarketable even though zoning laws generally do not by themselves.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, because zoning restrictions can never affect marketable title.
choices.rationale: The mere existence of zoning may not, but an existing violation and enforcement notice can create an unacceptable risk.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because marketable title concerns only defects in the chain of ownership.
choices.rationale: Marketability also concerns encumbrances and risks of litigation materially affecting the property.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, but only if the buyer proves that the seller intentionally concealed the violation.
choices.rationale: The contractual duty to convey marketable title does not depend on fraudulent concealment.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether an existing zoning violation subject to an enforcement order makes title unmarketable.
explanation.governingRule: Marketable title is title free from reasonable doubt and material risk of litigation. Although zoning ordinances themselves ordinarily do not make title unmarketable, an existing violation may.
explanation.application: The addition violates the setback rule and is subject to a removal order, creating a direct and material burden the buyer did not agree to accept.
explanation.commonTrap: Failing to distinguish a general zoning restriction from a present violation of it.
explanation.memoryAid: Zoning rule alone may be fine; a live violation is a title problem.

questions.subject: Real Property
questions.subtopic: Equitable conversion - risk of loss before closing
questions.stem: A buyer and seller entered an enforceable contract for the sale of a house. The contract was silent about risk of loss, and the jurisdiction follows the traditional common-law doctrine of equitable conversion without a contrary statute. Before closing, the house was destroyed by a wildfire without fault by either party. The seller was ready and able to convey marketable title on the closing date.
questions.call: Who bears the loss under the traditional rule?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The seller, because legal title remains with the seller until delivery of the deed.
choices.rationale: Under equitable conversion, the buyer is treated as equitable owner after an enforceable contract, despite the seller retaining legal title as security.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The seller, because risk of loss always follows possession.
choices.rationale: The traditional equitable-conversion rule places risk on the equitable owner even before possession, absent contrary law or agreement.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Neither party, because destruction automatically rescinds every land-sale contract.
choices.rationale: Traditional equitable conversion does not automatically rescind the contract when the seller can convey title.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The buyer, because the buyer became the equitable owner when the enforceable contract was formed.
choices.rationale: Under the stated traditional rule, accidental casualty loss falls on the buyer between contract and closing.

explanation.testedIssue: Who bears accidental casualty risk after an enforceable land-sale contract under traditional equitable conversion.
explanation.governingRule: Under the traditional doctrine of equitable conversion, the buyer is regarded as equitable owner once an enforceable contract is formed and generally bears the risk of accidental loss before closing, unless contract or statute provides otherwise.
explanation.application: The contract was enforceable, no contrary allocation applies, and the seller can convey title. The buyer therefore bears the wildfire loss under the specified rule.
explanation.commonTrap: Following legal title alone and overlooking the question’s express equitable-conversion assumption.
explanation.memoryAid: After contract, equity treats buyer as owner and seller as secured holder.

questions.subject: Real Property
questions.subtopic: Purchase-money mortgage - priority over prior judgment lien
questions.stem: A creditor obtained and properly recorded a judgment lien against a debtor. Several months later, the debtor purchased land from a seller. As part of the same transaction, the debtor gave the seller a mortgage securing the unpaid purchase price, and the seller promptly recorded the mortgage. The jurisdiction follows the usual priority rules and has no contrary statute. The debtor later defaulted on both obligations.
questions.call: Which lien has priority in the purchased land?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The judgment lien, because it was recorded before the purchase-money mortgage.
choices.rationale: A purchase-money mortgage ordinarily receives special priority over earlier claims against the purchaser that attach when the purchaser acquires title.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The seller’s purchase-money mortgage, because it financed the debtor’s acquisition of the land.
choices.rationale: The mortgage and acquisition occurred as one transaction, giving the purchase-money mortgage priority over the debtor’s preexisting judgment lien.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The judgment lien, because a seller-financed mortgage is always subordinate to all recorded liens.
choices.rationale: Seller purchase-money mortgages generally receive especially strong priority, not automatic subordination.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Neither lien, because a judgment debtor may not acquire land subject to existing debts.
choices.rationale: The debtor can acquire land; the issue is the order in which the liens attach and rank.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a seller’s purchase-money mortgage has priority over a judgment lien recorded against the buyer before acquisition.
explanation.governingRule: A purchase-money mortgage given as part of the acquisition transaction generally has priority over liens against the purchaser that attach to the property at the moment title is acquired. A seller’s purchase-money mortgage ordinarily has priority over other purchase-money lenders as well.
explanation.application: The debtor could not acquire the property without simultaneously subjecting it to the seller’s mortgage. The earlier judgment lien attaches behind that mortgage.
explanation.commonTrap: Applying first-in-time priority without recognizing purchase-money priority.
explanation.memoryAid: Acquisition money enters title first in priority.

questions.subject: Real Property
questions.subtopic: Foreclosure - omitted junior mortgagee
questions.stem: A landowner gave Bank 1 a first mortgage and later gave Bank 2 a second mortgage on the same land. After default, Bank 1 properly foreclosed but failed to name or serve Bank 2 in the foreclosure action. A purchaser bought the land at the foreclosure sale. Bank 2's mortgage was valid and recorded.
questions.call: What is the effect of Bank 1's failure to join Bank 2?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Bank 2’s mortgage is extinguished because every junior lien is automatically eliminated by a senior foreclosure.
choices.rationale: A junior interest is ordinarily extinguished only if its holder is properly made a party to the foreclosure.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The entire foreclosure is void, and Bank 1 loses its mortgage.
choices.rationale: Failure to join a junior lienholder generally preserves that junior lien rather than invalidating the senior foreclosure entirely.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Bank 2’s mortgage remains on the land, and the purchaser takes subject to it.
choices.rationale: Because Bank 2 was omitted, its interest was not foreclosed and continues to burden the property.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Bank 2 becomes personally liable for Bank 1’s unpaid debt.
choices.rationale: Omission from foreclosure does not transfer the senior debt to the junior mortgagee.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a junior mortgage is extinguished when its holder is omitted from a senior foreclosure.
explanation.governingRule: A foreclosure terminates junior interests only if their holders are properly joined. An omitted junior lien remains attached to the property, and the foreclosure purchaser takes subject to it.
explanation.application: Bank 2 held a recorded junior mortgage but received no party notice. Its lien therefore survives the sale.
explanation.commonTrap: Assuming that lien priority alone eliminates procedural joinder requirements.
explanation.memoryAid: Junior lien omitted means junior lien survives.

questions.subject: Real Property
questions.subtopic: Adverse possession - tacking through privity
questions.stem: A possessor openly, notoriously, exclusively, and adversely occupied a strip of land for six years. The possessor then conveyed by deed all of the possessor's claimed interest in the strip to a buyer, who continued the same adverse possession for five more years. The record owner did not interrupt either possession. The statutory period is 10 years.
questions.call: Has the buyer acquired title by adverse possession?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because the successive adverse possessors were in privity and may tack their periods of possession.
choices.rationale: The voluntary deed created privity, allowing the six- and five-year periods to combine beyond the statutory period.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, because every adverse possessor must personally occupy for the entire statutory period.
choices.rationale: Successive possessors in privity may ordinarily tack their periods.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because adverse possession can never be transferred by deed.
choices.rationale: A voluntary transfer of the possessory claim is the classic basis for privity and tacking.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, but only if the record owner had actual knowledge of every day of occupation.
choices.rationale: Open and notorious possession supplies constructive notice; actual daily knowledge is unnecessary.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether successive adverse possessors may combine their possession periods after a voluntary conveyance.
explanation.governingRule: Successive adverse possessors may tack their periods when they are in privity, usually created by a voluntary transfer such as a deed, will, or inheritance.
explanation.application: The first possessor conveyed the claimed interest by deed, and the buyer continued the same adverse possession. The combined 11 years exceeds the statutory period.
explanation.commonTrap: Assuming that adverse possession is always personal and nontransferable.
explanation.memoryAid: Voluntary transfer creates privity; privity permits tacking.

questions.subject: Torts
questions.subtopic: Assault - plaintiff must perceive imminent harmful or offensive contact
questions.stem: During an argument, a man silently pointed an unloaded handgun at a woman from behind, intending to frighten her when she turned around. Before the woman noticed the man or the gun, a police officer intervened and took the gun. The woman learned what had happened only afterward and suffered no physical contact.
questions.call: Is the man liable to the woman for assault?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because the man intended to place the woman in apprehension of harmful contact.
choices.rationale: Intent alone is insufficient; the plaintiff must actually experience apprehension of imminent contact.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because pointing a gun always constitutes assault even if the target is unaware of it.
choices.rationale: Assault protects against apprehension, so an unaware target has not suffered the required invasion.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because the woman did not perceive the threatened contact while it was imminent.
choices.rationale: She never became aware of the gun before intervention, so she experienced no apprehension of imminent contact.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, because an unloaded gun cannot create assault liability.
choices.rationale: An unloaded gun can cause reasonable apprehension if the plaintiff perceives it and reasonably believes it can be used; the absence of perception is decisive here.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether attempted intimidation constitutes assault when the plaintiff never perceives the threat.
explanation.governingRule: Assault requires intent to cause harmful or offensive contact or imminent apprehension of such contact, and the plaintiff must actually experience reasonable apprehension of imminent contact.
explanation.application: The man intended fright, but the woman did not see the gun or otherwise perceive the threat until after it had ended.
explanation.commonTrap: Focusing only on the defendant’s intent and conduct instead of the plaintiff’s contemporaneous apprehension.
explanation.memoryAid: Assault requires awareness before the threat is over.

questions.subject: Torts
questions.subtopic: Defense of property - deadly force not permitted solely to protect property
questions.stem: A homeowner saw an unarmed trespasser attempting to break into an unoccupied storage shed in the homeowner's backyard. The homeowner reasonably believed that the trespasser intended to steal gardening equipment but did not believe that any person faced death or serious bodily harm. Without warning, the homeowner fired a rifle and wounded the trespasser.
questions.call: Is the homeowner liable for battery?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because a landowner may use any force necessary to prevent a felony on the land.
choices.rationale: Deadly force is not privileged merely to prevent theft or protect property when no person faces serious danger.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because deadly force may not be used solely to protect property from an unarmed trespasser.
choices.rationale: The homeowner lacked a reasonable belief of a threat to human life or serious bodily harm, so the shooting was excessive and unprivileged.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because a trespasser assumes the risk of any force used by the property owner.
choices.rationale: Trespass does not authorize disproportionate or deadly force.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, but only if the gardening equipment was worth less than the trespasser’s medical expenses.
choices.rationale: The privilege does not turn on a comparison of property value and damages; deadly force is categorically unavailable solely to protect property.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a property owner may intentionally use deadly force to prevent theft from an unoccupied structure.
explanation.governingRule: A person may use reasonable nondeadly force to protect property after an appropriate request to desist when feasible, but may not use deadly force solely to protect property unless the circumstances also justify defense of self or others.
explanation.application: The homeowner believed only that property was threatened and had no basis to fear death or serious bodily harm. The rifle shot was not privileged.
explanation.commonTrap: Treating a trespass or intended felony as automatic permission for deadly force.
explanation.memoryAid: Property permits reasonable force, not deadly force by itself.

questions.subject: Torts
questions.subtopic: Land possessors - artificial conditions dangerous to child trespassers
questions.stem: A landowner knew that neighborhood children frequently entered an unfenced vacant lot to play. The landowner stored an abandoned industrial freezer on the lot with its door attached and a defective latch that could trap a child inside. Young children could not appreciate the suffocation risk, and removing the door would have been easy and inexpensive. A six-year-old entered the freezer, became trapped, and was injured.
questions.call: Is the landowner likely liable for negligence?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because a land possessor never owes a duty to a trespassing child.
choices.rationale: The attractive-nuisance doctrine may impose a duty regarding dangerous artificial conditions when its requirements are met.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, because the freezer itself did not cause children to enter the lot.
choices.rationale: The artificial condition need not be what initially attracts the child under the modern rule.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, because landowners are strictly liable for every injury to a child caused by an artificial condition.
choices.rationale: Liability rests on negligence elements, including knowledge, unreasonable risk, the child’s inability to appreciate the danger, and a favorable burden-risk balance.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, because the landowner knew children were likely to trespass, the artificial condition posed a grave hidden risk, and the burden of making it safe was slight.
choices.rationale: The facts satisfy the traditional elements for liability to child trespassers involving an artificial condition.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a landowner owes a duty to protect likely child trespassers from a dangerous artificial condition.
explanation.governingRule: A land possessor may be liable for harm to child trespassers from an artificial condition when children are likely to trespass, the possessor knows of an unreasonable risk, the children cannot appreciate it because of youth, the utility and burden of eliminating it are slight compared with the risk, and reasonable care is not used.
explanation.application: The owner knew children entered, the freezer posed a severe concealed danger, a six-year-old could not appreciate it, and removing the door was easy.
explanation.commonTrap: Assuming the condition must lure children onto the land or that liability is strict.
explanation.memoryAid: Known child trespass plus hidden artificial danger plus easy fix creates duty.

questions.subject: Torts
questions.subtopic: Standard of care - child engaged in an adult activity
questions.stem: A 14-year-old operated a high-powered motorboat on a crowded public lake. The child had received basic instruction but carelessly turned across another boat's path, causing a collision. The injured passenger sued the child for negligence. The jurisdiction follows the traditional rule for children engaged in adult activities.
questions.call: What standard of care applies?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The standard of a reasonably prudent adult operating a motorboat under similar circumstances.
choices.rationale: Operating a powerful motorized vehicle is a dangerous activity ordinarily undertaken by adults, so the child is held to the adult standard.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The standard of a reasonably careful 14-year-old with similar intelligence and experience.
choices.rationale: That child standard ordinarily applies to child activities, but not to dangerous adult activities such as operating motor vehicles.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Strict liability, because minors are not permitted to operate dangerous machinery.
choices.rationale: The question concerns negligence, and minority does not itself create strict liability.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No standard of care, because children under 16 cannot be liable for negligence.
choices.rationale: Children can be negligent; the applicable standard varies with age and activity.

explanation.testedIssue: What negligence standard applies to a minor operating a powerful motorboat.
explanation.governingRule: A child is ordinarily judged by a reasonable child of similar age, intelligence, and experience, but a child engaged in a dangerous activity normally undertaken only by adults is held to the reasonable-adult standard.
explanation.application: Operating a high-powered motorboat on a public lake is an adult activity, so the ordinary adult operator standard governs.
explanation.commonTrap: Automatically applying the flexible child standard without examining the nature of the activity.
explanation.memoryAid: Adult activity means adult care standard.

authorityNotes: Traditional negligence standard for children and adult activities

questions.subject: Torts
questions.subtopic: Res ipsa loquitur - accident ordinarily implying negligence
questions.stem: A pedestrian walking beside a warehouse was struck by a sealed barrel that fell from an upper loading platform. At the time, the warehouse company controlled the platform and all barrels stored there. The pedestrian did not enter the property or contribute to the occurrence. There is no direct evidence identifying the employee or precise act that caused the barrel to fall.
questions.call: May the pedestrian rely on res ipsa loquitur to reach the jury on breach?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because res ipsa loquitur is unavailable whenever the plaintiff cannot identify the negligent employee.
choices.rationale: The doctrine is designed for circumstances in which direct evidence of the precise negligent act is unavailable.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, because the pedestrian must conclusively exclude every possible nonnegligent explanation.
choices.rationale: The plaintiff generally must show that negligence is the more likely explanation, not eliminate every theoretical alternative.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, because barrels ordinarily do not fall from controlled loading platforms without negligence, and the company controlled the probable causes.
choices.rationale: The occurrence, control, and absence of plaintiff contribution support an inference of negligence.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, and the doctrine requires the court to direct a verdict for the pedestrian.
choices.rationale: Res ipsa ordinarily permits, but does not compel, the factfinder to infer negligence.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a falling barrel from a defendant-controlled platform supports a res ipsa loquitur inference.
explanation.governingRule: Res ipsa loquitur permits an inference of negligence when the event ordinarily would not occur without negligence, the likely causes were within the defendant’s control, and the plaintiff did not contribute.
explanation.application: A properly managed barrel does not ordinarily fall onto a passerby, the warehouse controlled the platform and barrels, and the pedestrian played no role.
explanation.commonTrap: Believing the doctrine requires identification of the specific negligent actor or creates automatic liability.
explanation.memoryAid: Unusual negligent-type event plus defendant control permits an inference.
